36.11 General Information

Schedule of Checks and Maintenance

To ensure the continued performance of your Filtermist system, the following daily, weekly and monthly checks should be carried out. The forms included in this book should be used to record these checks, together with comments on any corrective action required.

A separate section for regular maintenance and unplanned repair work is also included and should be completed as appropriate.

Any failure found should be reported to the person responsible for maintenance work. Please refer to the Troubleshooting section of the LEV Manual for more information.

It is important these records are kept up to date as they will help ensure the system is operating correctly and provide evidence that HSE guidelines are being followed.

Daily Checks

  • Check that the unit is turned on and operational.
  • Once the unit is running, check the status of the airflow monitor (if fittedairﬂow monitor (if ﬁtted).

Weekly Checks

  • Check all grilles and pre-filtersﬁlters within the extraction ductwork and ensure they are clear.
  • Check that the machine enclosure is clearing.
  • Check that the hoods (if applicable) are in the correct position to ensure that contaminants are being cleared from within the working zone.
  • Check the position of airflow regulator controls airﬂow regulator controls. These will have been set and marked during the commissioning process.

Monthly Checks

  • Check any interconnecting ductwork for any damage or leaks and check that all ductwork connections are secure.
  • Check that the drain hose is unrestricted and not submerged. Empty the collection container if one is used.
  • Check that any ‘U’ bend drain (if fittedﬁtted) is functioning correctly. Follow similar checks for the drain hose.
  • Check for excessive vibration or unusual noises from the Filtermist unit.
  • Check the capture hoods (if ﬁtted) for damage or blockage.
  • Check the condition of after ﬁlter (if ﬁtted).
  • Check the capture hoods (if fitted) for damage or blockage.
  • Check the condition of after filter (if fitted).

2.1    Fundamental Safety Precautions

Thoroughly read the machine manual and safety labels affixed to the machine

▲    Before installing, operating, maintaining, servicing, or repairing the machine, carefully study this manual to fully understand its contents, operational methods, and potential causes of personal injury or machine damage.

▲    Safety labels on the machine indicate areas of potential hazards. Read and comprehend these warnings thoroughly.

Machine Application Scope

▲    This machine is a CNC horizontal lathe designed for metal cutting. Do not use it for purposes outside its original design specifications.

▲    Perform machining only within the machine’s rated working range.

▲    Use the fixtures provided with the machine whenever possible. If alternative fixtures or tooling equipment are selected, ensure the spindle speed does not exceed the fixture/tooling’s maximum allowable speed. Verify proper workpiece clamping before operation. Our company shall not be liable for injuries or equipment damage

Follow all workshop regulations and operate the machine safely

▲    Operation is typically performed by a single operator. If multiple operators are required, clarify roles, sequences, and actions in advance, and execute under unified command.

▲    Do not operate the machine under fatigue, illness, emotional distress, or under the influence of alcohol/medication. Impaired judgment may lead to malfunctions, injuries, or equipment damage.

Wear Appropriate Safety Gear

▲    Operators must wear dedicated workwear, safety shoes, caps, and safety goggles to ensure personal protection.

▲    Avoid gloves, loose ties, scarves, or dangling accessories during operation to prevent entanglement in moving/rotating parts, which may cause severe injuries.

Maintain a Safe Workspace

▲    Keep the machine area clean and free of clutter. Immediately clean spills of lubricants or coolants to avoid slip hazards.

▲    Ensure sufficient clearance around the machine.

▲    Do not place tools, parts, or objects on machine guards, doors, slides, or other areas that may interfere with operation.

Fire Prevention

▲    Lubricants and coolants used in the machine are flammable. Handle with extreme caution.

▲    Ensure fire prevention and extinguishing equipment is available near the machine.

▲    Keep flammable materials and high-temperature devices away from the machine.

Unauthorized Modifications Prohibited

▲    Structural modifications to the machine are strictly prohibited without prior written approval from our company.

▲    We shall not be held liable for injuries or damage caused by unauthorized alterations or usage beyond permitted specifications.

Electrical and Maintenance Safety

▲    Ensure the machine’s electrical system is grounded in compliance with the GB5226.1-2002 standard before operation. Do not touch controllers or switches with wet hands. High-voltage components (e.g., electrical cabinets, transformers, motors) must only be inspected or repaired by qualified personnel.

▲    Avoid connecting power cables from interference-generating devices (e.g., welding machines, high-frequency hardening equipment) to the control panel

▲    Clearly label the machine as “FAULTY — DO NOT USE” during repair.

▲    All maintenance must be performed by certified technicians after disconnecting the power supply and switching the main breaker to [OFF].

2.2    Operational Precautions

Pre-Use Precautions for Initial Operation

▲    While the machine is equipped with multiple safety devices to protect operators and equipment, do NOT rely solely on these safeguards. Strictly adhere to operational safety protocols to prevent hazards caused by device malfunctions or failures. Thoroughly review this manual and ensure operators are professionally trained and certified before use.

▲    Remove all transportation locks on protective doors before operation. Failure to do so may damage the machine.

▲    Prior to initial operation, thoroughly remove all preservative oil from the machine surfaces using kerosene and lubricate all designated lubrication points in accordance with the manufacturer's specifications.

▲    Do NOT use deteriorated or contaminated lubricants.

Safety Device Verification

▲    Verify proper functionality of all safety devices before operation.  Do NOT operate the machine until repairs are completed if any device is faulty:

▲    Check the accuracy of X/Z-axis soft limits and mechanical limit switches before machining.

▲    Confirm the emergency stop button is responsive and reliable.

Operational Guidelines

▲    Gloves are prohibited during operation.

▲    Do NOT open doors during machine operation.

▲    Do NOT touch moving parts with hands or tools.

▲    For long bar stock extending beyond the spindle, install warning signs or protective barriers to prevent injuries.

▲    Immediately stop the machine and perform maintenance if abnormal noise or vibration occurs. Resume operation only after troubleshooting.

▲    Memorize the location of the emergency stop button to enable instant activation during emergencies.

▲    Remove chips promptly. 

▲    Always power off the machine before cleaning the machining area. Use dedicated cleaning tools to remove chips.  DO NOT handle chips with bare hands—they are high-temperature and sharp-edged, posing severe injury risks.

▲    Implement strict protocols for machine organization, routine maintenance, and workspace cleanliness to ensure optimal performance and longevity.

▲    CNC (Computer Numerical Control) and PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) parameters are factory-preset and typically require no adjustments.

▲    If modifications are necessary: Fully understand the functional implications of each parameter. Perform changes ONLY under the supervision of our certified technicians.

▲    DO NOT alter technical parameters beyond the machine’s rated specifications, including: Maximum spindle speed, Rapid traverse rates of tool posts and Other critical thresholds.

▲    Our company shall not be held liable for equipment damage, injuries, or accidents resulting from unauthorized modifications.

2.3    Waste Disposal

▲    Waste lubricants and cutting fluids generated during replacement must not be directly discharged onto the ground or into drainage systems. All waste liquids must be properly stored or disposed of at designated locations specified by environmental protection authorities.

▲    For non-recyclable toxic, hazardous, or non-degradable waste (e.g., electrical components, rubber parts, lubricants) generated during final machine scrapping, disposal must comply with relevant regulations.

3.1    Main Structure

The L2 Series CNC Lathe adopts a flat guideway saddle structure, with the X-axis carriage featuring a 30° inclined configuration. Capable of optional hydraulic tailstock integration, it represents the standard configuration universally adopted in modern entry-level and full-function CNC lathes. The machine delivers high rigidity, exceptional machining flexibility, robust chip protection, efficient chip evacuation, and user-friendly operation. Its modular structural design and fully enclosed guarding ensure operational reliability. 

3.2    Key Characteristics

3.2.1 The bed is cast from premium HT300 gray cast iron in an integral box-type structure, optimized through finite element analysis and reinforced with strategically distributed ribs. This design delivers exceptional rigidity and thermal stability.  The right or rear chip removal configuration provides large chip capacity and enhances flexibility for workshop layout optimization and space utilization.

3.2.2 Equipped with a flat guideway bed and a 30° inclined X-axis carriage, this compact structure delivers superior rigidity and excellent thermal stability.

3.2.3 The L2/L2L models adopt a box-type high-rigidity triple-disc servo turret, providing rapid tool change and excellent chip resistance. The L2LD model utilizes a Taiwan BMT45 or German Schaudt VDI30 12-station power turret, which can be flexibly configured with either power tool holders or fixed tool holders. The power tool holders are equipped with high-performance servo motors capable of reaching rotational speeds up to 4,000 rpm, enabling them to effortlessly handle machining processes such as turning, milling, drilling, and tapping of components.

3.2.4 The guideways employ high-quality linear roller guides from Taiwan, which significantly improve rigidity and service life. Featuring high-speed movement, superior precision retention, and ease of maintenance, these guideways are specifically designed for precision machining applications.

3.2.5 Imported precision ball screws are used on both axes, providing fast, stable, and highly accurate movement. Each screw adopts a pre-tensioned, double-supported structure with dedicated bearings. The pre-tensioning, precisely calculated and assembled, effectively minimizes machining accuracy deviations caused by thermal expansion of the screws.

3.2.6 The spindle assembly adopts a high-rigidity A2-6 spindle unit, with a maximum spindle speed of 4,000 rpm. The high-rigidity symmetrical spindle box is equipped with an 8-inch hydraulic chuck, ensuring high spindle stiffness and minimal thermal deformation.

3.2.7 The main motor is standardly configured with an 11 kW high-performance spindle servo motor, delivering high spindle output torque, high rotational speed, and a wide speed regulation range.

3.2.8 This series offers two models for selection: one with a hydraulic tailstock and one without. The tailstock sleeve taper hole can accommodate a Morse No. 4 center, while the tailless model provides more unobstructed chip evacuation space.

3.2.9 The machine features two lubrication methods: grease lubrication and oil lubrication. Spindle bearings and screw bearings use long-life grease lubrication for extended service and maintenance-free operation. Screw pairs and guideways are lubricated by an automatic centralized oil system, ensuring reliable and adequate lubrication.

3.2.10 Equipped with LED lighting—energy-efficient, high-performance, and long-lasting. Features a three-color status indicator for real-time machine operation monitoring, enabling intuitive visual management on the shop floor.

3.2.11· This machine features a compact design, sleek appearance, and fully enclosed guards for reliable protection, along with efficient chip evacuation. It is equipped with high-strength tempered safety glass windows, bright work lighting, and an ergonomic operating height for optimal comfort.

Before initiating machining, ensure the following elements are confirmed:

1)    Workpiece clamping method

2)    Suitable tools meeting process requirements

3)    Proper cutting parameters and CNC program aligned with process specifications

The operating procedures for the L2 series, including optional functions, are described below:

4.1    Workpiece Clamping Methods

4.1.1 The workpiece clamping method of this machine tool generally utilizes a hollow hydraulic chuck.

4.1.2 The spindle is standardly equipped with an 8-inch hydraulic chuck. 

4.1.3 During assembly, ensure the draw tube is fully threaded into the hydraulic cylinder and chuck. Improper connections may cause machine damage.

4.1.4 The total system pressure of the hydraulic power unit can be adjusted via the regulating screw on the hydraulic pump, typically set to 40 kgf/cm². Hydraulic cylinder operation
The cylinder operates using hydraulic pressure, which can be adjusted by rotating the regulating knob of the pressure-reducing valve. The maximum allowable working pressure for the 8-inch hollow power chuck must not exceed 26 kgf.

4.1.5 The through-hole chuck design enables bar stock feeding for continuous machining.

4.1.6 For long bar stock processing, integrate with a bar feeder.

4.1.7 Optional clamping solutions include solid hydraulic chucks, pneumatic chucks, collet chucks, or custom fixtures. Unauthorized fixture modifications are prohibited. Damage or safety incidents caused by such actions are not covered by the manufacturer’s liability.

4.2    Workpiece Support Methods

4.2.1 For CNC machine tools without hydraulic tailstock functionality, the workpiece support method is as follows.

The spindle chuck clamps one end of the workpiece and rotates it, while the cutting tool mounted on the turret performs machining operations. The recommended maximum length-to-diameter ratio is 1:5.

Long Bar Processing Integrate with a long bar feeder for continuous machining of extended workpieces.

4.2.2 For CNC machine tools equipped with a single chuck and hydraulic tailstock function, the workpiece support method is as follows:

a) Chuck-Tailstock Support (One-Clamp-One-Center)

Clamp one end of the workpiece with the left mechanical spindle chuck and support the opposite end with the tailstock center. The workpiece rotates for turret-based cutting.

b) Dual Tailstock Support (Two-Center Method)

Mount a center on the left spindle end and support both workpiece ends with tailstock centers. The workpiece rotates for cutting. Critical Notes: Insecure workpiece fixation may cause tool damage or machine failure. Ensure the spindle labyrinth seal remains effective to prevent coolant ingress and machine damage.

4.3    Workpiece Loading/Unloading Techniques

Workpiece handling includes manual and automated methods.

4.3.1 Manual Operation

Directly load/unload workpieces into/from the chuck by hand.

4.3.2 Gantry/Articulated Robot Handling

A method in which a gantry robot or articulated robot automatically picks up unfinished parts from the material tray and loads them into the chuck for machining, or unloads finished parts from the chuck and places them into the tray.

This method enables continuous automation, improves efficiency, reduces labor, and supports high-volume production.

4.3.3 Bar Feeder Method

Automatically feed bar stock through the spindle draw tube bore from the machine’s left side.
Repeatedly perform cutting, machining, and feeding of bar stock in sequence.

4.3.4 Workpiece Catcher Method

Collect finished parts cut from continuous bar machining and eject them outside the machine.
Typical Application: Combined with a bar feeder on the supply side for seamless production.

4.4    Chip Handling Methods

To discharge chips from the machine or remove chips near the workpiece and cutting tool, the following methods are available:

4.4.1 Manual Chip Disposal

A manual chip collection box is installed within the machine bed cavity. During chip removal, manually scrape chips out of the machine using tools.

4.4.2 Chip Conveyor System

An optional chain-type automatic chip conveyor can be installed in the machine bed cavity. The conveyor supports two discharge orientations: right-side discharge or rear discharge. During machining, chips falling into the conveyor are automatically expelled from the machine into a chip collection trolley positioned below the conveyor outlet.

4.4.3 Chip Removal at Machining Zone

Chip removal and tooltip cooling are typically achieved via coolant spray nozzles mounted on the tool turret. In specific cases, high-pressure air blast may be employed.

4.4.4 High-Pressure Chip Flushing

For materials prone to continuous chips or deep-hole machining, an optional high-pressure cutting oil pump can be installed to flush chips using pressurized cutting oil.

4.5    Cutting Fluid Application Guidelines

During cutting operations, tool temperature rises significantly due to high-intensity pressure processing. To mitigate cutting resistance, suppress built-up edge (BUE) formation, cool the tool, improve surface finish on precision-machined workpieces, enhance dimensional accuracy, prolong tool life, and facilitate chip evacuation, the use of appropriate cutting fluids is critical. 

4.5.1 Cutting Fluid Delivery

The machine is equipped with an independent coolant tank beneath the bed. A coolant pump delivers cutting fluid from the tank to the machining zone through turret-mounted nozzles for cooling and chip flushing.

4.5.2 Cutting Fluid Storage

The cutting fluid is stored in an independent coolant tank located beneath the machine bed, with a capacity of approximately 90 liters.
Maintaining adequate fluid levels prevents machine thermal expansion and ensures stable machining accuracy.

4.5.3 Cutting Fluid Selection

Select fluids based on workpiece material and machining requirements. Improper selection may degrade tool life and part accuracy.

4.5.4 The coolant tank filter must be cleaned frequently to ensure that cutting fluid flows adequately and smoothly through the coolant lines. 

4.5.6 When connecting the main power supply to the machine, the coolant pump must be removed and lifted out of the coolant tank. Activate the coolant pump and verify that the impeller rotation direction aligns with the arrow indicator on the pump housing. If the rotation direction is incorrect, reverse the phase sequence of the power supply and retest until proper rotation is confirmed.  Failure to correct the rotation direction will result in reduced coolant flow and premature pump failure.

4.6    Optional Accessories

4.6.1 Automatic Tool Setter

The machine can be equipped with a precision automatic tool-setting system. This device automatically detects tool tip positions under program control, enabling rapid generation of the initial machining position in the control system. Additionally, periodic re-detection of tool tip positions compensates for tool wear, ensuring consistent machining accuracy during prolonged operations.

4.6.2 Bar Feeder

For installation and operation of the bar feeder, refer to the electrical circuit diagrams provided with the machine and the bar feeder’s dedicated user manual.

4.6.3 Pneumatic Automatic Part Catcher

The machine may be optionally configured with a pneumatic part-catching system. The sequence is as follows:

1）After completing all machining operations on a workpiece held by the chuck, the system program activates a cylinder-driven mechanism. This rotates the collection box clockwise to position it beneath the workpiece. The cutoff tool on the turret severs the finished part, which then falls into the collection box.

2) Under CNC program control, the pneumatic cylinder rotates the collection box counterclockwise to tilt the workpiece onto a conveyor belt. The belt, driven by a gear motor, transports the workpiece from the right side of the machine into a secondary collection bin. Once multiple parts have accumulated in the bin, an operator can manually retrieve them.

4.6.4 In-Process Workpiece Inspection System

The machine can be optionally equipped with an in-process workpiece inspection system. The wireless inspection receiver is mounted above the spindle, and the contact-type probe is installed on the turret’s boring tool holder. After the workpiece machining is completed, the turret can be program-controlled to move the probe into contact with the workpiece. The system detects deviation values of the workpiece and automatically compensates for them in the CNC program, ensuring machining accuracy.

4.6.5 High-Pressure Coolant Pump System

The high-pressure coolant pump system can be selected based on application requirements. It is designed for chip-breaking and tool tip cooling during part machining, effectively prolonging tool life while ensuring optimal compatibility with automated loading/unloading mechanisms.

Article title: Section B General Outline and Specifications
Article body:
This chapter describes the construction, external appearance, dimensions, features, and specifications of each machine component.

•    Use the equipment only for its intended purpose and in accordance with the specified methods.

•    Failure to comply may result in personal injury, equipment malfunction, or adverse effects on the machine’s service life.
Subcategory: Section B General Outline and Specifications

Article title: Machine Tool Manual L2 - User Notice
Article body:
User Notice

Before operating this machine, be sure to read the contents of this user manual to ensure proper usage.  If you have any questions, please contact the nearest authorized dealer or our company directly.

Store this user manual near the machine for easy access and safekeeping.

Strictly adhere to the safety precautions outlined in this manual and the warning labels attached to the machine.  Violations may result in severe personal injury or damage to the equipment.

Note: Some illustrations in this manual depict components with covers or doors removed to clarify detailed operations.  However, all safety covers and doors must be securely installed during actual operation.

Comply with all applicable laws and regulations in the country or region where the machine is used.

This machine is intended for operators who possess fundamental knowledge of machining equipment.

Our company shall not be held liable for accidents caused by untrained individuals or those lacking sufficient expertise in machine operation, maintenance, or programming.  When replacing parts, use only components specified by our company.

Accidents resulting from the use of non-approved parts are not our responsibility.  Unauthorized parts may compromise machine performance, safety, and lead to injuries or equipment damage.

While our company does not specify cutting fluids, consult thoroughly with cutting fluid manufacturers and exercise caution in selection. 

We are not liable for accidents related to cutting fluids.

All rights, including copyrights, to this manual belong to our company.  Technical drawings, specifications, and content herein may not be disclosed, reproduced, translated, or stored in electronic devices without prior written consent.

Content in this manual is subject to change without prior notice.

Company names and product names mentioned in this manual are trademarks or registered trademarks of their respective owners.

The NT 200 Series Turning Center is a high-performance machine independently developed by our company.  It is designed to clamp metal workpieces onto the spindle, where the spindle servo motor the rotation of the workpiece, while the tool turret—controlled by the CNC (Computer Numerical Control) system—moves simultaneously to carry out high-efficiency turning operations (with milling capabilities on some models). Through the coordinated motion of the main spindle, sub-spindle, X-axis, and Z-axis, the machine can complete complex and precision machining tasks—including turning, milling, threading, and drilling—in a single setup. This product adopts a modular design, allowing flexible configurations to meet diverse conditions and requirements. 

The main variants include:

a)    L2 CNC Lathe

b)    L2L CNC Lathe

c)    L2LD CNC Lathe with Live Tooling Turret

★ Final specifications are subject to the purchase contract or technical agreement.

The following usage and machining methods are prohibited, as they may cause personal injury or machine tool failure.

Always refer to the Machine Tool Safety Guidelines for safe operation practices.

•    Operate within the permissible rotational speed range of the spindle, chuck, and hydraulic cylinder.

•    Failure to comply may result in workpiece ejection, leading to personal injury or machine damage. 

•    Always close the front door during operation.

•    Do not operate with covers removed or movable parts exposed.

•    Violation may cause entanglement in rotating parts or pinching by moving components, resulting in severe injury.

•    Never operate the machine after removing or modifying safety devices, protective covers, or safety windows.

•    Violation may cause entanglement in rotating parts or pinching by moving components, resulting in severe injury.

•    Do not operate the machine when bar stock protrudes from the rear of the spindle.

•    Rotating extended bar stock may vibrate excessively, causing injury or machine damage.

Never mount cutting tools on the spindle side for machining.

Example:
Prohibited: Installing workpieces on the turret and tools on the spindle for milling operations.
Violation may cause machine damage.

During workpiece clamping with the chuck:

•    Do not feed the turret in Z/X-axis directions while the spindle is stationary for keyway machining or planning operations.

•    Such operations generate abnormal thrust on feed-system ball screws, potentially causing mechanical failure.

•    Avoid using compressed air to remove chips inside the machine.

•    Scattered chips may infiltrate unintended areas of the inclined structure, leading to machine malfunctions.

When machining flammable materials or substances generating hazardous byproducts:

•    Consult specialists and implement protective gear, safety devices, or environmental controls.

•    Negligence may result in fire accidents or chemical exposure injuries.

•    Operators must receive proper NC lathe operation training and guidance.

•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

•    Maintenance personnel must undergo NC lathe operation/maintenance training.

•    Qualified electricians or crane operators should be employed as needed.

•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

This chapter provides a concise explanation of the machine’s intended purpose, operational methods, and key features.

•    Please use it in accordance with the intended purpose and the specified usage instructions.

•    Failure to comply may result in personal injury, machine malfunction, or damage. Thoroughly read and understand the [Safety Guidelines] before operation.

•    Inadequate comprehension of these guidelines may lead to personal injury, machine malfunction, or damage.

1.1    Spindle Construction

The spindle structure and critical dimensions are detailed in [Figure B1-1: Spindle Key Dimensions]. 
Lubrication: Pre-filled with Klüber Germany's high-performance, long-life grease (optimized for high-speed applications) at factory. Maintenance-free under normal operating conditions.

1.2    Spindle Motor Speed/Torque Output Curves

The spindle motor can be configured with different control systems, including: Syntec-compatible motors，Fanuc-series motors and Siemens-drive motors.

The curves do not account for machine tool efficiency. The transmission ratio between the spindle motor and spindle is 1:1.32.

Subcategory: Section B General Outline and Specifications

Article body:
6.1    Hydraulic Chuck Maintenance

6.1.1 Regular disassembly and cleaning of the chuck is required. During reassembly, apply oil as needed. 

6.1.2 Inspection Items and Cycle for Chuck Maintenance: 

•    Disassembly and cleaning: Every 1000 hours 

•    Clamping accuracy check: Every 500 hours 

•    Stroke check: Every 500 hours 

•    Chuck jaw cleaning: Every 8 hours  

•    Main jaw lubrication: Every 8 hours   

6.1.3 Grease the chuck using a grease gun (if there is insufficient grease in the main jaw, clamping force will decrease). 

Recommended grease: Molybdenum disulfide grease.

Lubrication Method

For lubrication procedures, refer to [Chapter D, 2.4 Lubricants]. 

When adding coolant to the chuck, ensure that the frequency of lubrication is increased, and always check that the grease is not exhausted. 

6.2    Hydraulic Cylinder Maintenance 

6.2.1 If oil leakage occurs, disassemble, clean, and replace the O-ring. 

6.2.2 During reassembly, ensure that the hydraulic piping is connected correctly. 

6.2.3 Inspection Items and Cycle:  

Cutting oil recovery port: Every 8 hours

Article title: Section C Machine Tool Transportation and Installation
Article body:
This chapter provides instructions in the sequence from machine installation to operation.

•    During collaborative work, ensure mutual coordination and verification.

•    Violation may cause entanglement in rotating parts or pinching by moving components, resulting in severe injury.

•    Thoroughly read and understand the [Safety Guidelines] before operation.

•    Failure to do so may result in personal injury or damage to the machine tool.

•    Install protective barriers and warning signs (e.g., "No Entry") in the work area.

•    Non-compliance may cause accidents involving nearby personnel.

•    Operators must wear safety helmets, safety shoes, and appropriate work attire.

•    Failure to comply may result in injuries such as cuts or impacts.
Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation and Installation

Article body:
•    Install the machine tool under appropriate environmental conditions.

•    Failure to comply may lead to malfunctions in the main unit or peripheral electrical systems, resulting in machine failure.

1.1    Installation Environment

NC lathes are equipped with precision components. To maximize performance and extend service life, proper environmental conditions must be maintained.

The machine must be installed indoors, protected from weather, with careful attention to ambient temperature, dust, vibration, and noise levels.

Environmental Conditions

•    Ambient Temperature: 0–35°C
•    Temperature Fluctuation: ≤1.1°C/min
•    Humidity: ≤75% (at 20°C)
•    Optimal Surrounding Temperature: 17–26°C
•    Daily Temperature Variation: Within ±2° Cover 24 hours
•    Vibration: ≤0.5 G

Notes on Environmental Setup

1)    Do not install the machine outdoors. 
Avoid direct sunlight or HVAC airflow.
Significant temperature variations will directly affect machining accuracy.

2)    Avoid locations with excessive vibration or noise from nearby equipment. machine failure and adversely impact machining precision.

3)    Avoid humid (≥76%), dusty, or foggy environments. This may lead to machine failure.

4)    Do not place unrelated items near the machine.

1.2    Foundation

To ensure optimal machining precision, the machine must be installed on a firm and stable foundation. 
Depending on the ground conditions at the installation site, the foundation should be constructed using standard methods for machine tool installation. (Refer to: Foundation Drawing)

Notes on Foundation Work

1)    Typically, the concrete thickness of the factory floor should be 300 mm.
2)    The bearing capacity must be ≥ 0.05 MPa.
3)    The foundation should extend approximately 300 mm beyond the machine base.
4)    When selecting the installation location, consider: Access for opening/closing the control cabinet；Space for the chip conveyor；Space for the cutting oil tank；and Maintenance clearance.

Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation and Installation

Article body:
•    Operators must hold valid qualifications for forklift, crane, and slinging operations.

•    Allowing untrained personnel to handle forklifts, cranes, or slinging equipment may result in personal injury or machine damage.

•    Do not use bent or frayed cables during lifting.

•    Improper cables or accessories may cause the machine to fall, leading to personal injury or machine damage.

2.1    Machine Handling with Forklift

2.1.1 The machine features an integrated base structure with pre-designed forklift pockets. Use these pockets for forklift handling during installation.

2.1.2 Clear obstacles from the transportation path and restrict unauthorized personnel access.  Operate the forklift at low speed— sudden acceleration, abrupt stops, or sharp turns may cause tipping of the machine or forklift.

2.1.3 Remove decorative panels at the positions shown in the diagram below.

2.1.4 Use a 5-ton or larger forklift and align the forks with the designated forklift pockets as illustrated.

2.1.5 After moving the machine to the target location, install the adjustment screws (included in the accessories) before grounding the machine. 

Place shim plates under the screws, ensuring the concave holes align with the screws. Adjust the screws to level the machine after grounding.

2.2    Machine Handling with Crane

Crane lifting for machine installation is highly hazardous and requires extreme caution.

2.2.1 Notes for Slinging Operations

(1) Remove the left/right side covers and install dedicated lifting lugs on the machine.

(2) Use sling ropes with a minimum diameter of 16 mm.

Machine weight: Approximately 3,200 kg.

(3) Prepare I-beam lifting tools (approx. 320 mm × 150 mm).

(4) The machine’s center of gravity is marked in the lifting diagram. 
Adjust with chain hoists if unbalanced.

(5) Lift slowly. After tensioning the sling ropes, reconfirm the slinging setup and center of gravity before fully raising the machine. 

Similarly, lower the machine gradually and verify the landing position before grounding.

After installation, remove the internal fixing tools (marked in red) securing the saddle and cross slide.

2.2.2 The lifting fixtures shown in the figure below shall be manufactured as follows.

(1) Left-side lifting fixture, two pieces in total.

(2) Right-side lifting fixture, two pieces in total.

2.2.3.2 L2L/L2LD Machine Hoisting Diagram
Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation and Installation

Article body:
Proper machine leveling is critical, as it directly affects machining accuracy and machine tool longevity.

When leveling the machine, remove the fixed shipping brackets (yellow), and clean the anti-rust coating from the X-axis and Z-axis sliding surfaces.

Follow the sequence below to adjust the machine level.

1)    Use a precision level with sensitivity of 0.02 mm/m to check the horizontal level. Place it on the base slide surface and check along both Z-axis and X-axis directions. 

2)    Adjust the leveling screws at the lower part of the base until the bubble in the level is centered.

＜Inspection Item＞
Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation and Installation

Article body:
•    Exercise extreme caution to prevent electric shock during electrical work. 

•    Electric shock may result in fatal injury.

•    Electrical work must be performed exclusively by licensed electricians.  Electric shock may result in fatal injury.

•    Open the rear electrical cabinet door of the machine tool. Thread the power cable through the cable entry hole into the cabinet. Connect the power cables in the sequence of A1, A2, A3, PE (Protective Earth).

•    Note that power capacity and cable specifications vary depending on the machine model. Insert the power cable from the left side of the main power cabinet. 

•    Verify L1, L2, L3 phases before connecting to the main switch.

•    Use a grounding wire (PE) with a cross-sectional area of ≥14 mm² and maintain grounding resistance below 100 ohms.

•    Do NOT share power sources with high-capacity equipment (e.g., welding machines, EDM machines).  Voltage fluctuations from such equipment may cause control system malfunctions.

•    Before wiring, ensure both the machine’s short-circuit protection switch and the air switch for power input are in the "OFF" position.

Article body:
To ensure efficient machine tool operation, operators must fully understand the machine's functions and performance and utilize them effectively.  This is critical.

Regular lubrication and inspection by operators help maintain the machine's precision and service life.

Follow the daily inspection checklist to perform inspections properly.

If any abnormalities are detected during inspection, report them to the equipment supervisor immediately.

The equipment supervisor will perform maintenance.  If the issue cannot be resolved, contact our company.

•    Do not allow oil spills on the floor or work area.

•    Immediately wipe up any spilled oil.

•    Slippery surfaces may cause falls.

2.1    Precautions During Storage

1)    Store oil indoors, protected from rain and direct sunlight.

2)    Ensure drum lids are tightly sealed to prevent dust and moisture ingress.

2.2    Precautions During Oil Replenishment 

1)    Use dedicated oil dispensers only—do not mix with other oils.

2)    Do not remove the filter screens from oil tank fill ports during replenishment.

3)    Avoid using degraded, aged, or contaminated oils.

4)    Do not mix oils from different manufacturers。

5)    If non-specified or mixed oils are introduced, immediately flush the tank and piping.

2.3    Precautions for Waste Oil Disposal

1)    Never dispose of waste oil indiscriminately.

2)    Entrust waste oil disposal only to licensed waste management providers.

2.4    Lubricant Top-Up

Lubrication and oil replenishment points on the machine tool are shown below.

If oil levels in sight glasses are low, replenish as specified in this section.

Note: The spindle lubrication system uses sealed grease and requires no replenishment.

2)    Chuck

2)    Do not mix oils from different manufacturers。
3)    If non-specified or mixed oils are used, immediately flush the tank and piping.

2.5    Cutting Fluids 

Cutting fluids are classified into two types: non-water-soluble cutting fluids and water-soluble cutting fluids. 

These fluids possess primary properties related to cutting performance, tool life, and machining surface accuracy, as well as secondary properties related to factors like corrosion resistance, safety, and cost-effectiveness, which make them easier to use.

Improper use of cutting fluids can lead to machine tool malfunctions. Please consider the following factors when selecting a cutting fluid:

(1) Primary Properties (Performance that Enhances Cutting Efficiency)

•    Reduces cutting resistance, eliminates built-up edge, and improves machining surface accuracy.

•    Lowers cutting temperature and extends tool life.

(2) Secondary Properties (User-Friendly Features) 

•    Prevents rust on both the workpiece and the machined part. 

•    Produces minimal smoke and mist.

•    Consistent quality (no separation or layering, with non-aging properties). 

•    Non-harmful to human health (including no unpleasant odors).

•    Does not affect coatings.

The user is responsible for preparing the cutting fluid. When adding fluid, monitor the oil level in the tank, typically filling it to about 3/4 of the tank's height.

3.1    Before Powering On 

•    If any inspection results are unsatisfactory, do not use the machine tool until repairs or fixes have been completed.  

•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

•    Please clean up any chips in a timely manner. 

•    Failure to properly manage chips may lead to malfunctions and poor machining results.

3.1.1 Inspect the area around the machine tool for abnormalities. 

1)    Check if external piping and hoses are detached. 

2)    Inspect the power cables for any broken wires or damage to the outer insulation.  

3)    Check for any damage to hydraulic or pneumatic hoses. 

4)    Ensure that all doors on the machine tool are properly closed.

3.1.2 Inspect the floor around the machine tool.  

1)    Check for any oil leaks: 

•    Cutting fluid, lubricating oil, hydraulic oil, etc.  

2)    Clear any obstacles from the work area.

•    Tools, fixtures, measuring instruments, materials, etc.    

3)    Clean up any chips that have fallen onto the floor.

3.1.3 Check that the total pressure gauge and two hydraulic operation pressure gauges on the hydraulic station show the correct readings.

3.2    Inspection After Operation Preparation

•    If any inspection results are unsatisfactory, do not operate the machine tool until repairs or fixes have been completed.  

•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

Confirm that the hydraulic unit pump operates with a normal sound. 

Verify the pressure of each hydraulic circuit.  

The total pressure of the hydraulic unit should be 4.0 MPa. 

Check whether the pressure of the chuck cylinder is appropriate: For an 8-inch chuck, pressure should be less than 26 kgf; For a 6-inch chuck, pressure should be less than 25 kgf. 

Tailstock pressure should be adjusted according to operating requirements. 

3) Confirm that the lubrication oil pump is functioning correctly. 
4) Check that the air conditioning unit on the electrical cabinet side is operating normally. 
5) Confirm that the switches and indicator lights on the control panel are functioning correctly. 
6) Check for any alarm indications on the system display screen.

•    If any inspection results are unsatisfactory, do not operate the machine tool until repairs or fixes have been completed. 

•    Failure to comply may cause workpieces or chuck jaws to be ejected, potentially leading to personal injury and machine damage.  

1)    Confirm installation of all protective covers.

2)    Confirm the installation of the turning tools and boring bars. 

3)    Verify the condition of the cutting edges and wear of the turning tools and boring bars. 

4)    Confirm the installation of the chuck jaws. 

5)    Check for any damage to the dust scraper or sliding protective covers.

To minimize the impact of heat generated during the initial automatic operation on workpiece machining accuracy, preheating of the spindle and all moving axes should be performed before automatic operation. 

When preparing the preheating program, in addition to spindle operation (1000 rpm) and rapid feed movements, please include a program that covers various functions. 

•    Caution If the machine has been idle for an extended period (over one week), suddenly running the spindle at high speeds could damage the spindle's oil film, leading to bearing wear.

In such cases, set the spindle speed to 300 rpm for about 30 minutes, then increase to 500 rpm for another 30 minutes for preheating. For detailed information about preheating operations, please contact the manufacturer.

•    During Automatic Operation Warning

•    If any inspection results are unsatisfactory, immediately stop the machine tool.

•    Do not operate the machine until repairs or fixes have been completed. 

•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

1)    Confirm the operational sound and vibration of the spindle during operation. 

2)    Confirm the sound of the spindle motor during operation.   

3)    Confirm the vibration during automatic operation. 

4)    Confirm the load status of the spindle motor (data can be monitored on the system display screen).

5)    Confirm the machining accuracy of the workpiece.

•    Do not use compressed air to clean the inside of the machine tool. 

•    Flying chips may enter unintended areas such as guideways, which can lead to machine malfunctions.  

Use a brush and cloth to clean the interior of the machine tool. 

Remove chips from the base and chip conveyor, disposing of them outside the machine, and transport.

chips from the chip cart to the designated disposal area.

Article body:
7.1    Classification of Machine Lubricants

The lubrication of this machine tool is divided into grease lubrication and oil lubrication.

7.1.1 Grease Lubrication

The bearings of this machine tool are lubricated with high-speed long-life grease, requiring no maintenance under normal operating conditions.

7.1.2 Oil Lubrication

The lubrication of linear guides, ball screw nuts, and transmission gears in the power tool turret is achieved through a centralized oil supply from an automatic lubrication pump. The system will trigger an alarm when the oil level in the pump’s reservoir is insufficient. Immediately refill the reservoir upon such an alarm.

7.1.3 Recommended lubricating oil: ISO32 mineral-based lubricating oil. Users can adjust the lubrication cycle according to demand. 

To ensure sufficient lubrication at all points, the manufacturer recommends setting the lubrication pump to operate every 30 minutes, with each cycle lasting no less than 10 seconds. If improper settings result in insufficient lubrication and cause machine damage, the company assumes no liability.

7.1.4 Precautions 

•    All lubricants must be clean and free from contamination. 
•    Regularly inspect the lubrication pump and replenish oil promptly. Replace contaminated oil immediately.

7.2    Lubrication Distribution Diagram 

7.3    Lubrication Unit 

The lubrication unit adopts a resistance-type electric oil pump. 

Lubrication intervals are adjustable.

Equipped with a liquid level sensor: a buzzer will alarm and send an error signal to the control system when oil is low.

D Daily Inspection Protocol

This section outlines mandatory inspection procedures during machine operation:

Conduct comprehensive machine diagnostics as fundamental maintenance.

Items not listed in the manual should be checked as part of routine maintenance.   

Refer to manuals for maintenance.

•    Thoroughly read and understand the [Safety Guidelines] before operation.

•    Failure to do so may result in personal injury or damage to the machine tool.

•    Be sure to thoroughly read the [Operation Manual] and [Program Manual] to master the operating methods.

•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.
Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation and Installation

Article body:
This section outlines the key points for regular maintenance and adjustments to maintain the machine tool's accuracy and longevity. 

Maintenance personnel should also refer to other manuals for specific maintenance procedures. 
For maintenance guidelines not covered in this manual, refer to manuals for maintenance.

•    When performing maintenance work, always disconnect the power supply before starting.  Contact with high-voltage electricity can be fatal.

•    Thoroughly read and understand the [Safety Guidelines] before operation.

•    Failure to understand the content thoroughly before use can result in personal injury, machine malfunctions and damage.

•    Maintenance personnel must fully understand and strictly adhere to the precautions for maintenance tasks. 

•    Failure to do so may lead to unforeseen injuries.

The primary objective of maintenance inspections is to detect any abnormalities in the machine early, preventing potential failures, thereby increasing machine uptime and productivity. 

To achieve this, it is essential to create a maintenance schedule based on the [Regular Inspection Checklist], and for maintenance personnel to perform inspections and maintenance according to the schedule. 

Additionally, it is important to consider feedback from daily inspectors and make any necessary adjustments. 

Besides this manual, maintenance should also refer to the operation manuals, electrical diagrams, and ladder diagrams.

Personnel Maintenance work involves various risks, and personnel must be attentive and careful while performing maintenance tasks.

Maintenance personnel should observe the following precautions: 

•    When working with electrical systems, always disconnect the power supply and lock the main switch. 

•    Failure to do so can lead to electric shocks, entanglement in rotating parts, or being caught in moving components. 

•    Always display a "Do Not Energize" warning during maintenance tasks. 

•    -Electrical system maintenance should only be carried out by personnel designated as responsible for maintenance by the factory. 

•    Performing electrical work without proper electrical safety knowledge can lead to electrocution risks. 

•    When working together, always confirm with others before proceeding with operations. 

•    Failure to confirm can result in electric shocks, entanglement in rotating parts, or being caught in moving components.   

•   When entering the machine for maintenance or inspection, always disconnect the power supply.

•    Performing maintenance or inspections with the power on increases the risk of unintended machine movements, which could lead to entanglement in rotating parts or injury from moving components.

•    Always wear protective equipment and appropriate work attire suited for the job. 

•    Failure to do so can lead to personal injuries such as cuts, bumps, and entanglement in rotating parts. 

•    During maintenance, ensure that a "Maintenance Work in Progress" sign is posted to inform others that maintenance is taking place. 

•    Failure to do so may result in accidental machine operation by personnel unaware of the ongoing maintenance, potentially causing injury. 

•    Use the proper tools for the maintenance tasks at hand. 

•    Using inappropriate tools can lead to personal injuries and machine damage. 

•    When handling heavy objects, use a crane. 

•    Failure to do so may result in losing balance and injury from falling objects. 

•    Never use frayed or knotted ropes.  Using damaged ropes can result in the object being dropped, leading to potential injury.

To conduct maintenance effectively, ensure the following preparations are made.

By properly preparing, even emergency repairs can be completed quickly. 

Consult with the operator responsible for the machine to understand any abnormal conditions.  

Investigate the situation, plan the scope of work, and establish the sequence of tasks. 

Familiarize yourself with the specifications, structure, and functionality of the maintenance points in advance. 

If working with other departments, ensure thorough discussions and coordination beforehand.   

If parts or consumables need to be replaced, make sure to have them prepared in advance.   
Prepare necessary tools, ladders, and step stools for the work.

Please perform inspections and maintenance according to the upcoming scheduled maintenance details, and cross-check with the daily inspection items.

The belt plays a crucial role in transferring the output from the spindle motor to the spindle. 

Because belt tension affects both cutting efficiency and service life, setting the correct tension is extremely important.

Over time, belts can lose tension due to elongation and wear from continuous operation, requiring regular inspection and adjustment.

To adjust the belt tension, loosen the four fixed screws on the motor base and use the adjustment bolts to modify the tension. 

There are two methods for measuring belt tension: the UNITTA sonic belt tension meter and the BANDO tension gauge.

Article title: Machine Tool Manual L3 Series CNC Lathe - User Notice
Article body:
USER NOTICE

Before operating this machine, be sure to read the contents of this user manual to ensure proper usage.  If you have any questions, please contact the nearest authorized dealer or our company directly.

Store this user manual near the machine for easy access and safekeeping.

Strictly adhere to the safety precautions outlined in this manual and the warning labels attached to the machine.  Violations may result in severe personal injury or damage to the equipment.

Note: Some illustrations in this manual depict components with covers or doors removed to clarify detailed operations.  However, all safety covers and doors must be securely installed during actual operation.

Comply with all applicable laws and regulations in the country or region where the machine is used.

This machine is intended for operators who possess fundamental knowledge of machining equipment.

Our company shall not be held liable for accidents caused by untrained individuals or those lacking sufficient expertise in machine operation, maintenance, or programming.  When replacing parts, use only components specified by our company.

Accidents resulting from the use of non-approved parts are not our responsibility.  Unauthor-ized parts may compromise machine performance, safety, and lead to injuries or equipment damage.

While our company does not specify cutting fluids, consult thoroughly with cutting fluid manufacturers and exercise caution in selection. 

We are not liable for accidents related to cutting fluids.

All rights, including copyrights, to this manual belong to our company.  Technical drawings, specifications, and content herein may not be disclosed, reproduced, translated, or stored in electronic devices without prior written consent.

Content in this manual is subject to change without prior notice.

Company names and product names mentioned in this manual are trademarks or registered trademarks of their respective owners.

2.1    Basic Precautions for Machine Use

Thoroughly read the machine manual and all safety labels affixed to the machine

▲    Before installing, operating, maintaining, servicing, or repairing the machine, carefully study this manual to fully understand its contents, operational methods, and potential causes of personal injury or machine damage.
▲    Safety labels on the machine indicate areas of potential hazards. Read and comprehend these warnings thoroughly.

Machine Application Scope

▲    This machine is a CNC horizontal lathe designed for metal cutting. Do not use it for pur-poses outside its original design specifications.
▲    Perform machining only within the machine’s rated working range.
▲    Use the fixtures provided with the machine whenever possible. If alternative fixtures or tooling equipment are selected, ensure the spindle speed does not exceed the fixture/tooling’s maximum allowable speed. Verify proper workpiece clamping before operation. 
▲    Our company shall not be liable for injuries or equipment damage.

▲    Follow all workshop regulations and operate the machine safely.
▲    Operation is typically performed by a single operator. If multiple operators are required, clarify roles, sequences, and actions in advance, and execute under unified command.
▲    Do not operate the machine under fatigue, illness, emotional distress, or under the in-fluence of alcohol/medication.  Impaired judgment may lead to malfunctions, injuries, or equipment damage.

Wear Appropriate Safety Gear

▲    Operators must wear dedicated workwear, safety shoes, caps, and safety goggles to ensure personal protection.
▲    Avoid gloves, loose ties, scarves, or dangling accessories during operation to prevent entanglement in moving/rotating parts, which may cause severe injuries.

Maintain a Safe Workspace

▲    Keep the machine area clean and free of clutter. Immediately clean spills of lubricants or coolants to avoid slip hazards.
▲    Ensure sufficient clearance around the machine.
▲    Do not place tools, parts, or objects on machine guards, doors, slides, or other areas that may interfere with operation.

Fire Prevention

▲    Lubricants and coolants used in the machine are flammable. Handle with extreme cau-tion.
▲    Ensure fire prevention and extinguishing equipment is available near the machine.
▲    Keep flammable materials and high-temperature devices away from the machine.

Unauthorized Modifications Prohibited

▲    Structural modifications to the machine are strictly prohibited without prior written approval from our company.
▲    We shall not be held liable for injuries or damage caused by unauthorized alterations or usage beyond permitted specifications.

Electrical & Maintenance Safety

▲    Ensure the machine’s electrical system is grounded in compliance with the GB5226.1-2002 standard before operation.
▲    Do not touch controllers or switches with wet hands.
▲    High-voltage components (e.g., electrical cabinets, transformers, motors) must only be inspected or repaired by qualified personnel.
▲    Avoid connecting power cables from interference-generating devices (e.g., welding ma-chines, high-frequency hardening equipment) to the control panel
▲    Clearly label the machine as “FAULTY — DO NOT USE” during repair.
▲    All maintenance must be performed by certified technicians after disconnecting the power supply and switching the main breaker to [OFF].

2.2    Operational Precautions

Pre-Use Precautions for Initial Operation

▲    While the machine is equipped with multiple safety devices to protect operators and equipment, do NOT rely solely on these safeguards. Strictly adhere to operational safety protocols to prevent hazards caused by device malfunctions or failures. Thoroughly re-view this manual and ensure operators are professionally trained and certified before use.
▲    Remove all transportation locks on protective doors before operation. Failure to do so may damage the machine.
▲    Clean antirust oil from machine surfaces with kerosene prior to initial startup. Lubricate all designated lubrication points as specified. 
▲    Do NOT use deteriorated or contaminated lubricants.

Safety Device Verification

Verify proper functionality of all safety devices before operation.  Do NOT operate the machine until repairs are completed if any device is faulty.
▲    Check the accuracy of X/Z-axis soft limits and mechanical limit switches before ma-chining.
▲    Confirm the emergency stop button is responsive and reliable.

Operational Guidelines

▲    Gloves are prohibited during operation.
▲    Do NOT open doors during machine operation.
▲    Do NOT touch moving parts with hands or tools.
▲    For long bar stock extending beyond the spindle, install warning signs or protective bar-riers to prevent injuries.
▲    Immediately stop the machine and perform maintenance if abnormal noise or vibration occurs. Resume operation only after troubleshooting.
▲    Memorize the location of the emergency stop button to enable instant activation during emergencies.
▲    Remove chips promptly. 
▲    Always power off the machine before cleaning the machining area. Use dedicated cleaning tools to remove chips.  DO NOT handle chips with bare hands—they are high-temperature and sharp-edged, posing severe injury risks.
▲    Implement strict protocols for machine organization, routine maintenance, and work-space cleanliness to ensure optimal performance and longevity.
▲    CNC (Computer Numerical Control) and PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) parame-ters are factory-preset and typically require no adjustments.
▲     If modifications are necessary: Fully understand the functional implications of
▲     each parameter. Perform changes ONLY under the supervision of our certified techni-cians.
▲    DO NOT alter technical parameters beyond the machine’s rated specifications, includ-ing: Maximum spindle speed, Rapid traverse rates of tool posts and Other critical thresholds.
▲    Our company shall not be held liable for equipment damage, injuries, or accidents re-sulting from unauthorized modifications.

2.3    Waste Disposal

▲    Waste lubricants and cutting fluids generated during replacement must not be directly discharged onto the ground or into drainage systems.  All waste liquids must be properly stored or disposed of at designated locations specified by environmental protection au-thorities.
▲    For non-recyclable toxic, hazardous, or non-degradable waste (e.g., electrical compo-nents, rubber parts, lubricants) generated during final machine scrapping, disposal must comply with relevant regulations.

3.1    Main Structure

The L3 series CNC lathe/turning center adopts a flat-guide saddle structure. The X-axis saddle features a 30° configuration, while the Y-axis models incorporate a 0°+30° inclined angle between the Y-axis and X-axis guides. The machine can be equipped with a sub-spindle or hydraulic tailstock (servo tailstock optional). The main spindle is an 11 kW belt-driven mechanical spindle (optional motorized spindle). When configured with a FANUC system, the subs-pindle is a 12.5 kW motorized spindle (13.2 kW for Siemens 828D/ShinDai 22T systems). The machine boasts high rigidity, flexible machining capabilities, reliable protection, efficient chip removal, and user-friendly operation. Its modular design and fully enclosed housing ensure compact dimensions and an aesthetically streamlined appearance.

Key Characteristics

3.2.1 The bed is cast from premium HT300 gray cast iron in an integral box-type structure, op-timized through finite element analysis and reinforced with strategically distributed ribs. This design delivers exceptional rigidity and thermal stability.  The right or rear chip removal config-uration provides large chip capacity and enhances flexibility for workshop layout optimization and space utilization.

3.2.2 The flat-guide bed and X-axis saddle with 30° inclination, combined with the Y-axis guides inclined at a 0°+30° angle, enable a compact footprint. The Y-axis achieves a maximum milling travel of ±50 mm.

3.2.3 Equipped with a high-quality BMT55 12-station live tool turret, the machine supports flex-ible combinations of power or fixed tool holders. Power tool holders are driven by high-performance servo motors, achieving speeds up to 6,000 RPM (★ Actual specifications de-pend on CNC system configuration; refer to purchase contracts or technical agreements). This enables seamless integration of turning, milling, drilling, and tapping operations.

3.2.4 All three axes utilize 35 mm-wide linear roller guides, ensuring high rigidity, extended ser-vice life, rapid motion, precision retention, and simplified maintenance—ideal for precision machining.

3.2.5 Imported precision ball screws are employed for all axes, delivering fast, stable, and high-precision movement. Each screw features pre-tensioned double-end support with specialized bearings. Precision-calculated pre-tension assembly minimizes machining errors caused by thermal expansion of the screws.

3.2.6 The main spindle adopts a high-rigidity A2-6 spindle with a double-row roller bearing structure, paired with a rigid symmetrical headstock and an 8-inch hydraulic chuck. This design ensures exceptional spindle rigidity and minimal thermal deformation.

3.2.7 The sub-spindle utilizes an A2-5 spindle and 6-inch hydraulic chuck, driven by a 12.5 kW built-in motorized spindle (13.2 kW when equipped with Siemens 828D/ShinDai 22T systems). The spindle achieves a maximum speed of 5,000 RPM with low vibration and stable rotation. Spindle cooling employs a forced oil circulation system.  After completing primary operations on the main spindle, the subspindle, driven by a servo motor, moves to clamp the workpiece during synchronized rotation of both spindles. The main spindle jaws then release the work-piece, allowing the subspindle to transfer it to the right side for secondary operations (e.g., milling, drilling) via the live tool turret.

3.2.8 Standard Programmable Hydraulic Tailstock: The tailstock body is fully movable, and its position can be programmed (forward/backward) using a graduated grid structure. It supports rapid feed/work feed switching as standard.

3.2.9 Optional Servo Tailstock: The tailstock body is fully movable, with programmable precision adjustment of thrust force. This configuration is ideal for slender shafts or workpieces re-quiring controlled clamping force.


3.2.8 The machine offers two lubrication modes: grease and oil lubrication.  Spindle bearings and screw bearings use long-life grease lubrication for extended service and maintenance-free operation. Screw pairs and guideways are lubricated by an automatic centralized oil system, ensuring reliable and adequate lubrication.


3.2.9 LED Lighting: Energy-efficient, high-brightness, and long-lasting illumination. Three-Color Status Indicator Lights: Provide real-time visualization of machine states (e.g., running, idle, alarm) for enhanced visual management.


3.2.10 The machine features a compact design, aesthetically streamlined enclosure, and fully enclosed protection for operational safety. Key ergonomic elements include: High-strength tempered safety glass windows for clear visibility; Bright LED work lights; Ergonomic operating height for reduced operator fatigue; Efficient chip evacuation system for uninterrupted production.

The following usage and machining methods are prohibited, as they may cause personal injury or machine tool failure.

Always refer to the Machine Tool Safety Guidelines for safe operation practices.

•    Operate within the rated rotation speeds of the spindle, chuck, and    pressure cylinder. Failure to comply may result in workpiece ejection, leading to personal injury or machine damage.
•    Always close the front door during operation.
•    Do not operate with covers removed or movable parts exposed.
•    Violation may cause entanglement in rotating parts or pinching by moving components, resulting in severe injury.
•    Never operate the machine after removing or modifying safety devices, protective co-vers, or safety windows.
•    Violation may cause entanglement in rotating parts or pinching by moving components, resulting in severe injury.
•    Do not operate the machine when bar stock protrudes from the rear of the spindle.
•    Rotating extended bar stock may vibrate excessively, causing injury or machine damage.

•    Never mount cutting tools on the spindle side for machining.
•    Example:
•    Prohibited: Installing workpieces on the turret and tools on the spindle for milling operations Violation may cause machine damage.
•    During workpiece clamping with the chuck：
•    Do not feed the turret in Z/X-axis directions while the spindle is stationary for keyway machining or planning operations.
•    Such operations generate abnormal thrust on feed-system ball screws, potentially causing mechanical failure.
•    Avoid using compressed air to remove chips inside the machine.
•    Scattered chips may infiltrate unintended areas of the inclined structure, leading to machine malfunctions.
•    When machining flammable materials or substances generating hazardous by-products:
•    Consult specialists and implement protective gear, safety devices, or environmental controls.
•    Negligence may result in fire accidents or chemical exposure injuries.
•    Operators must receive proper NC lathe operation training and guidance.
•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.
•    Maintenance personnel must undergo NC lathe operation/maintenance training.
•    Qualified electricians or crane operators should be employed as needed.
•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

Article title: Section C Machine Tool Transportation and Installation
Article body:
This chapter provides instructions in the sequence from machine installation to operation.

•    During collaborative work, ensure mutual coordination and verification.
•    Violation may cause entanglement in rotating parts or pinching by moving components, resulting in severe injury.
•    Thoroughly read and understand the [Safety Guidelines] before operation.
•    Failure to do so may result in personal injury or damage to the machine tool.
•    Install protective barriers and warning signs (e.g., "No Entry") in the work area.
•    Non-compliance may cause accidents involving nearby personnel.
•    Operators must wear safety helmets, safety shoes, and appropriate work attire.
•    Failure to comply may result in injuries such as cuts or impacts.
Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation & Installation

Article body:
•    Operators must hold valid qualifications for forklift, crane, and slinging operations.
•    Allowing untrained personnel to handle forklifts, cranes, or slinging equipment may result in personal injury or machine damage.
•    Do not use bent or frayed cables during lifting.
•    Improper cables or accessories may cause the machine to fall, leading to personal injury or machine damage.

2.1    Machine Handling with Forklift

2.1.1 The machine features an integrated base structure with pre-designed forklift pockets. Use these pockets for forklift handling during installation.

2.1.2 Clear obstacles from the transportation path and restrict unauthorized personnel ac-cess.  Operate the forklift at low speed—sudden acceleration, abrupt stops, or sharp turns may cause tipping of the machine or forklift.

2.1.3 Remove decorative panels at the positions shown in the diagram below.

2.1.4 Use a 5-ton or larger forklift and align the forks with the designated forklift pockets as illustrated

2.1.5 After moving the machine to the target location, install the adjustment screws (included in the accessories) before grounding the machine. 

Place shim plates under the screws, ensuring the concave holes align with the screws. Adjust the screws to level the machine after grounding.

2.2    Machine Handling with Crane

Crane lifting for machine installation is highly hazardous and requires extreme caution.

2.2.1 Notes for Slinging Operations

(1) Remove the left/right side covers and install dedicated lifting lugs on the machine.

(2) Use sling ropes with a minimum diameter of 16 mm.
Machine weight: Approximately 4,400 kg.

(3) Prepare I-beam lifting tools (approx. 320 mm × 150 mm).

(4) The machine’s center of gravity is marked in the lifting diagram. 
Adjust with chain hoists if unbalanced.

(5) Lift slowly. After tensioning the sling ropes, reconfirm the slinging setup and center of gravity before fully raising the machine. 

Similarly, lower the machine gradually and verify the landing position before grounding.
After installation, remove the internal fixing tools (marked in red) securing the saddle and cross slide.

2.2.2 The lifting fixtures shown in the figure below shall be manufactured as follows.

(1) Left-side lifting fixture, two pieces in total.

(2) Right-side lifting fixture, two pieces in total.

2.2.3 Machine Lifting Schematic Diagram

Article body:
•    Exercise extreme caution to prevent electric shock during electrical work. 
•    Electric shock may result in fatal injury.
•    Electrical work must be performed exclusively by licensed electricians.  Electric shock may result in fatal injury.
•    Open the rear electrical cabinet door of the machine tool. Thread the power cable through the cable entry hole into the cabinet. Connect the power cables in the sequence of A1, A2, A3, PE (Protective Earth).
•    Note that power capacity and cable specifications vary depending on the machine model. Insert the power cable from the left side of the main power cabinet. 
•    Verify L1, L2, L3 phases before connecting to the main switch.
•    Use a grounding wire (PE) with a cross-sectional area of ≥14 mm² and maintain grounding resistance below 100 ohms.
•    Do NOT share power sources with high-capacity equipment (e.g., welding machines, EDM machines).  Voltage fluctuations from such equipment may cause control system malfunctions.
•    Before wiring, ensure both the machine’s short-circuit protection switch and the air switch for power input are in the "OFF" position.

Article body:
To ensure efficient machine tool operation, operators must fully understand the machine's functions and performance and utilize them effectively.  This is critical.

Regular lubrication and inspection by operators help maintain the machine's precision and service life.

Follow the daily inspection checklist to perform inspections properly.

If any abnormalities are detected during inspection, report them to the equipment supervisor immediately.

The equipment supervisor will perform maintenance.  If the issue cannot be resolved, contact our company.

Do not allow oil spills on the floor or work area.
Immediately wipe up any spilled oil.
Slippery surfaces may cause falls.


2.1    Precautions During Storage

(1) Store oil indoors, protected from rain and direct sunlight.
(2) Ensure drum lids are tightly sealed to prevent dust and moisture ingress.

2.2    Precautions During Oil Replenishment 

(1) Use dedicated oil dispensers only—do not mix with other oils.
(2) Do not remove the filter screens from oil tank fill ports during replenishment.
(3) Avoid using degraded, aged, or contaminated oils.
(4) Do not mix oils from different manufacturers.
(5) If non-specified or mixed oils are introduced, immediately flush the tank and piping.

2.3    Precautions for Waste Oil Disposal

(1) Never dispose of waste oil indiscriminately.
(2) Entrust waste oil disposal only to licensed waste management providers.

2.4    Lubricant Top-Up

If oil levels in sight glasses are low, replenish as specified in this section.
Note: The spindle lubrication system uses sealed grease and requires no replenishment.

2.5    Cutting Fluids 

Cutting fluids are classified into two types: non-water-soluble cutting fluids and water-soluble cutting fluids. 

These fluids possess primary properties related to cutting performance, tool life, and machining surface accuracy, as well as secondary properties related to factors like corrosion resistance, safety, and cost-effectiveness, which make them easier to use.

Improper use of cutting fluids can lead to machine tool malfunctions. Please consider the following factors when selecting a cutting fluid:

(1) Primary Properties (Performance that Enhances Cutting Efficiency)
•    Reduces cutting resistance, eliminates built-up edge, and improves machining sur-face accuracy.
•    Lowers cutting temperature and extends tool life.

(2) Secondary Properties (User-Friendly Features) 
•    Prevents rust on both the workpiece and the machined part. 
•    Produces minimal smoke and mist.
•    Consistent quality (no separation or layering, with non-aging properties). 
•    Non-harmful to human health (including no unpleasant odors).
•    Does not affect coatings.

The user is responsible for preparing the cutting fluid. When adding fluid, monitor the oil level in the tank, typically filling it to about 3/4 of the tank's height.

This section outlines mandatory inspection procedures during machine operation:

Conduct comprehensive machine diagnostics as fundamental maintenance.

For commercially available components without Daily Inspection Protocol refer to manuals for maintenance.

•    Thoroughly read and understand the [Safety Guidelines] before operation.
•    Failure to do so may result in personal injury or damage to the machine tool.
•    Be sure to thoroughly read the [Operation Manual] and [Program Manual] to master the operating methods.
•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

Personnel Maintenance work involves various risks, and personnel must be attentive and careful while performing maintenance tasks.

Maintenance personnel should observe the following precautions: 

•    When working with electrical systems, always disconnect the power supply and lock the main switch. 
•    Failure to do so can lead to electric shocks, entanglement in rotating parts, or being caught in moving components. 
•    Always display a "Do Not Energize" warning during maintenance tasks. 
•    Electrical system maintenance should only be carried out by personnel designated as responsible for maintenance by the factory. 
•    Performing electrical work without proper electrical safety knowledge can lead to electrocution risks.   
•    When working together, always confirm with others before proceeding with operations. Failure to confirm can result in electric shocks, entanglement in rotating parts, or being caught in moving components.   
•    When entering the machine for maintenance or inspection, always disconnect the power supply. Performing maintenance or inspections with the power on increases the risk of unintended machine movements, which could lead to entanglement in rotating parts or injury from moving components.

•    Always wear protective equipment and appropriate work attire suited for the job. 
•    Failure to do so can lead to personal injuries such as cuts, bumps, and entangle-ment in rotating parts. 
•    During maintenance, ensure that a "Maintenance Work in Progress" sign is posted to inform others that maintenance is taking place. 
•    Failure to do so may result in accidental machine operation by personnel unaware of the ongoing maintenance, potentially causing injury. 
•    Use the proper tools for the maintenance tasks at hand. 
•    Using inappropriate tools can lead to personal injuries and machine dam-age. 
•    When handling heavy objects, use a crane. 
•    Failure to do so may result in losing balance and injury from falling objects. 
•    Never use frayed or knotted ropes.  Using damaged ropes can result in the object being dropped, leading to potential injury.

Please perform inspections and maintenance according to the upcoming scheduled maintenance details, and cross-check with the daily inspection items.

The belt plays a crucial role in transferring the output from the spindle motor to the spindle. Since belt tension affects cutting efficiency and tool life, it is important to set the correct tension. 

Over time, belts can lose tension due to elongation and wear from continuous operation, requiring regular inspection and adjustment.

To adjust the belt tension, loosen the four fixed screws on the motor base and use the adjustment bolts to modify the tension. 

There are two methods for measuring belt tension: the UNITTA ultrasonic belt tension meter and the BANDO tension meter. Below is the procedure for measuring belt tension using the BANDO tension meter shown below.  

When measuring the belt tension between the spindle motor and spindle, apply force (P in Newtons) to the center of the belt span, and adjust the deflection (L in millimeters) as shown in the table below.
