6.1    Hydraulic Chuck Maintenance

6.1.1 Regular disassembly and cleaning of the chuck is required.
During reassembly, apply oil as needed. 

6.1.2 Inspection Items and Cycle for Chuck Maintenance: 
•    Disassembly and cleaning: Every 1000 hours 
•    Clamping accuracy check: Every 500 hours 
•    Stroke check: Every 500 hours 
•    Chuck jaw cleaning: Every 8 hours  
•    Main jaw lubrication: Every 8 hours   

6.1.3 Grease the chuck using a grease gun (if there is insufficient grease in the main jaw, clamping force will decrease). Recommended grease: Molybdenum disulfide grease.
Lubrication procedures

For lubrication procedures, refer to [Chapter D, 2.4 Lubricants]. 

When adding coolant to the chuck, ensure that the frequency of lubrication is increased, and always check that the grease is not exhausted. 

6.2    Hydraulic Cylinder Maintenance 

6.2.1 If oil leakage occurs, disassemble, clean, and replace the O-ring. 

6.2.2 During reassembly, ensure that the hydraulic piping is connected correctly. 

6.2.3 Inspection Items and Cycle:  
Cutting oil recovery port: Every 8 hours

The L3 Series Turning Center is a high-performance machine independently developed by our company.  It is designed to clamp metal workpieces onto the spindle, where the spindle servo motordrives the rotation of the workpiece. Simultaneously, the tool turret, controlled by the CNC (Computer Numerical Control) system, moves to perform highly efficient turning and milling operations. 


Through the coordinated motion of the main spindle, sub-spindle, X-axis, Z-axis, and Y-axis, the machine can complete complex and precision machining tasks—including turning, milling, threading, and drilling—in a single setup. This product adopts a modular design, allowing flexible configurations to meet diverse conditions and requirements. 

The main variants include:
a. ET-205 CNC Lathe
b. ET-205D Turning Center
c. ET-205S Twin-Spindle CNC Lathe
d. ET-205SD Twin-Spindle Turning Center
e. ET-205Y Y-Axis Turning Center
d. ET-205SY Twin-Spindle Y-Axis Turning Center

★ Final specifications are subject to the purchase contract or technical agreement.

Before initiating machining, ensure the following elements are confirmed:

(1) Workpiece clamping method.

(2) Suitable tools meeting process requirements.

(3) Proper cutting parameters and CNC program aligned with process specifications.

The operating procedures for the L3 series, including optional functions, are described below:

4.1    Workpiece Clamping Methods

4.1.1 The standard clamping method utilizes a through-hole hydraulic chuck.

4.1.2 The main spindle is equipped with an 8-inch hydraulic chuck, while the subspindle uses a 6-inch hydraulic chuck.

4.1.3 During assembly, ensure the draw tube is fully threaded into the hydraulic cylinder and chuck. Improper connections may cause machine damage.

4.1.4 The total system pressure of the hydraulic power unit can be adjusted via the regulating screw on the hydraulic pump, typically set to 40 kgf/cm². The cylinder operating pressure can be adjusted by rotating the pressure relief valve's regulating knob. The maximum operating pressure of the 8-inch through-hole power chuck must not exceed 26 kgf/cm²; the 6-inch through-hole power chuck must not exceed 20 kgf/cm².

4.1.5 The through-hole chuck design enables bar stock feeding for continuous machining.

4.1.6 For long bar stock processing, integrate with a bar feeder.

4.1.7 Optional clamping solutions include solid hydraulic chucks, pneumatic chucks, collet chucks, or custom fixtures.

★     Unauthorized fixture modifications are prohibited. Damage or safety incidents caused by such actions are not covered by the manufacturer’s liability.

4.2    Workpiece Support Methods

For Dual-Spindle/Dual-Chuck Configurations

4.2.1 Single-Chuck Support

Clamp one end of the workpiece with the main spindle chuck on the left side. Rotate the work-piece for primary machining using tools mounted on the turret. Upon completing the first oper-ation, the NC system controls the right-side subspindle to move leftward toward the work-piece. The subspindle’s hydraulic chuck then clamps the machined outer diameter of the workpiece. After the left-side hydraulic chuck releases, the NC system retracts the subspindle to the right to a predetermined position. Secondary machining is performed on the opposite side of the workpiece using back-facing tools on the turret, thereby finishing the second operation.

4.2.2 Dual-Chuck Support

For long shaft-type workpieces, both ends can be clamped simultaneously by left and right hydraulic chucks. The two spindles rotate synchronously to ensure machining accuracy.

4.2.3 The subspindle’s W-axis can emulate a servo tailstock when paired with compatible fix-tures and centers. For specific configurations, consult fixture suppliers or the manufacturer.

4.2.4 Long Bar Processing

Integrate with a long bar feeder for continuous machining of extend-ed workpieces.For Single-Chuck with Tailstock Configurations

4.2.5 Single-Chuck Support

Clamp one end of the workpiece with the left main spindle chuck. Rotate the workpiece for machining using turret-mounted tools.  The recommended maximum length-to-diameter ratio is 1:5.

4.2.6 Chuck-Tailstock Support (One-Clamp-One-Center)

Clamp one end of the workpiece with the left mechanical spindle chuck and support the op-posite end with the tailstock center. The workpiece rotates for turret-based cutting.

4.2.7 Dual Tailstock Support (Two-Center Method)

Mount a center on the left spindle end and support both workpiece ends with tailstock centers. The workpiece rotates for cutting. Critical Notes: Insecure workpiece fixation may cause tool damage or machine failure. Ensure the spindle labyrinth seal remains effective to prevent coolant ingress and machine damage.

4.3    Workpiece Loading/Unloading Techniques

Workpiece handling includes manual and automated methods.

4.3.1 Manual Operation

Directly load/unload workpieces into/from the chuck by hand.

4.3.2 Gantry/Articulated Robot Handling

Automated loading/unloading using gantry or articulated robots: Robots transfer raw work-pieces from trays to the chuck or remove finished parts into trays.

This method enables continuous automation, improves efficiency, reduces labor, and sup-ports high-volume production.

4.3.3 Bar Feeder Method

Automatically feed bar stock through the spindle draw tube bore from the machine’s left side.
Repeatedly perform cutting, machining, and feeding of bar stock in sequence.

4.3.4 Workpiece Catcher Method

Collect finished parts cut from continuous bar machining and eject them outside the machine.
Typical Application: Combined with a bar feeder on the supply side for seamless production.

4.4    Chip Handling Methods

To discharge chips from the machine or remove chips near the workpiece and cutting tool, the following methods are available:

4.4.1 Manual Chip Disposal

A manual chip collection box is installed within the machine bed cavity. During chip removal, manually scrape chips out of the machine using tools.

4.4.2 Chip Conveyor System

An optional chain-type automatic chip conveyor can be installed in the machine bed cavity. The conveyor supports two discharge orientations: right-side discharge or rear discharge. During machining, chips falling into the conveyor are automatically expelled from the machine into a chip collection trolley positioned below the conveyor outlet.

4.4.3 Chip Removal at Machining Zone

Chip removal and tooltip cooling are typically achieved via coolant spray nozzles mounted on the tool turret. In specific cases, high-pressure air blast may be employed.

4.4.4 High-Pressure Chip Flushing

For materials prone to continuous chips or deep-hole machining, an optional high-pressure cutting oil pump can be installed to flush chips using pressurized cutting oil.

4.5    Cutting Fluid Application Guidelines

During cutting operations, tool temperature rises significantly due to high-intensity pressure processing. To mitigate cutting resistance, suppress built-up edge (BUE) formation, cool the tool, improve surface finish on precision-machined workpieces, enhance dimensional accuracy, prolong tool life, and facilitate chip evacuation, the use of appropriate cutting fluids is critical.

4.5.1 Cutting Fluid Delivery

The machine is equipped with an independent coolant tank beneath the bed. A coolant pump delivers cutting fluid from the tank to the machining zone through turret-mounted nozzles for cooling and chip flushing.

4.5.2 Cutting Fluid Storage

The cutting fluid is stored in the 90-liter-capacity independent coolant tank located below the bed. 
Maintaining adequate fluid levels prevents machine thermal expansion and ensures stable machining accuracy.

4.5.3 Cutting Fluid Selection

Select fluids based on workpiece material and machining requirements. Improper selection may degrade tool life and part accuracy.

4.5.4  The coolant tank filter must be cleaned frequently to ensure that cutting fluid flows adequately and smoothly through the coolant lines. 

4.5.6 When connecting the main power supply to the machine, the coolant pump must be removed and lifted out of the coolant tank. Activate the coolant pump and verify that the impeller rotation direction aligns with the arrow indicator on the pump housing. If the rotation direction is incorrect, reverse the phase sequence of the power supply and retest until proper rotation is confirmed.  Failure to correct the rotation direction will result in reduced coolant flow and premature pump failure.

4.6    Optional Accessories

4.6.1 Automatic Tool Setter

The machine can be equipped with a precision automatic tool-setting system. This device automatically detects tool tip positions under program control, enabling rapid generation of the initial machining position in the control system. Additionally, periodic re-detection of tool tip positions compensates for tool wear, ensuring consistent machining accuracy during prolonged operations.

4.6.2 Bar Feeder

For installation and operation of the bar feeder, refer to the electrical circuit diagrams provided with the machine and the bar feeder’s dedicated user manual.

4.6.3 Pneumatic Automatic Part Catcher

The machine may be optionally configured with a pneumatic part-catching system. The sequence is as follows:


1）After completing all machining operations on a workpiece held by the chuck, the system program activates a cylinder-driven mechanism. This rotates the collection box clockwise to position it beneath the workpiece. The cutoff tool on the turret severs the finished part, which then falls into the collection box.

2) Under CNC program control, the pneumatic cylinder rotates the collection box counter-clockwise to tilt the workpiece onto a conveyor belt. The belt, driven by a gear motor, trans-ports the workpiece from the left side of the machine into a secondary collection bin. Once multiple parts have accumulated in the bin, an operator can manually retrieve them.

4.6.4 In-Process Workpiece Inspection System

The machine can be optionally equipped with an in-process workpiece inspection system. The wireless inspection receiver is mounted above the spindle, and the contact-type probe is installed on the turret’s boring tool holder. After the workpiece machining is completed, the turret can be program-controlled to move the probe into contact with the workpiece. The system detects deviation values of the workpiece and automatically compensates for them in the CNC program, ensuring machining accuracy.

4.6.5 High-Pressure Coolant Pump System

The high-pressure coolant pump system can be selected based on application requirements. It is designed for chip-breaking and tool tip cooling during part machining, effectively prolonging tool life while ensuring optimal compatibility with automated loading/unloading mechanisms.

Article body:
8.1    Classification of Machine Lubricants

The lubrication of this machine tool is divided into grease lubrication and oil lubrication.

8.1.1 Grease Lubrication

The bearings of this machine tool are lubricated with high-speed long-life grease, requiring no maintenance under normal operating conditions.

The bevel gears in the machine’s self-manufactured tool turret center module are lubricated with high-speed long-life grease. The grease must be replenished every 1,000 hours or 6 months.

8.1.2 Oil Lubrication

The lubrication of linear guides, ball screw nuts, and transmission gears in the power tool turret is achieved through a centralized oil supply from an automatic lubrication pump. The system will trigger an alarm when the oil level in the pump’s reservoir is insufficient. Immediately refill the reservoir upon such an alarm.

8.1.3 Oil Lubricant Specifications

Use ISO VG32 to VG46 petroleum-based lubricating oil. Users may adjust the lubrication pump’s operating interval based on requirements.

To ensure sufficient lubrication at all points, the manufacturer recommends setting the lubrication pump to operate every 30 minutes, with each cycle lasting no less than 10 seconds the manufacturer shall not be held responsible for machine damage caused by improper user settings leading to inadequate lubrication.

8.1.4 Precautions

•    All lubricants must be clean and free from contamination. 
•    Regularly inspect the lubrication pump and replenish oil promptly. Replace con-taminated oil immediately.

8.2    Lubrication Diagram (L3Y/L3SY Models)

8.3    Lubrication Unit (L3Y/L3SY Models)

The lubrication unit employs a volumetric electric oil pump.
Lubrication intervals are adjustable.

Equipped with a liquid level sensor: a buzzer will alarm and send an error signal to the control system when oil is low.

Article body:
Install the machine tool under appropriate environmental conditions.

Failure to comply may lead to malfunctions in the main unit or peripheral electrical systems, resulting in machine failure.

1.1    Installation Environment

NC lathes are equipped with precision components.  To maximize performance and extend service life, proper environmental conditions must be maintained.
The machine must be installed indoors, protected from weather, with careful attention to am-bient temperature, dust, vibration, and noise levels.

Conditions

•    Ambient Temperature: 0–35°C
•    Temperature Fluctuation: ≤1.1°C/min
•    Humidity: ≤75% (at 20°C)
•    Optimal Surrounding Temperature: 17–26°C
•    Daily Temperature Variation: Within ±2°C over 24 hours
•    Vibration: ≤0.5 G

Environmental Considerations

1)    Do not install outdoors or in areas exposed to direct sunlight or HVAC airflow. Signifi-cant temperature variations will directly affect machining accuracy. 

2)    Avoid locations with excessive vibration or noise from nearby equipment. Machine fail-ure and adversely impact machining precision.

3)    Avoid humid (≥76%), dusty, or foggy environments. This may lead to machine failure.

4)    Do not place unrelated items near the machine.

1.2    Foundation

To ensure optimal machining accuracy, the machine tool must be installed on a solid foundation.

Depending on the ground conditions at the installation site, the foundation should be constructed using standard methods for machine tool installation.

(Refer to: Foundation Drawing)

1)    Typically, the concrete thickness of the factory floor should be 300 mm.
2)    The bearing capacity must be ≥ 0.05 MPa.
3)    The foundation should extend approximately 300 mm beyond the machine base.
4)    When selecting the installation location, consider: Access for opening/closing the control cabinet; Space for the chip conveyor; Space for the cutting oil tank; and Maintenance clearance.

1.3    L3SY Foundation Drawing 

1.3.1  L3Y Foundation Drawing 

1.3.1 L3SY Foundation Drawing

Article body:
Proper machine leveling is critical, as it directly affects machining accuracy and ma-chine tool longevity.

Preparation Steps: Remove the internal fixing tools (yellow) from the machine. Wipe off the rust-preventive coating applied to the sliding surfaces of the X-axis and Z-axis.

Follow the sequence below to adjust the machine level.

(1) Place a pre-calibrated level (0.02 mm/m sensitivity) on the base sliding surface, aligning it with the Z-axis and X-axis directions.

(2) Adjust the leveling screws at the lower part of the base until the bubble in the level is centered.

Inspection Items

•    Level inspection … Perform every 1000 hours.
Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation & Installation

Article body:
For the lubrication of machine tool oils and cutting oils, refer to Chapter D for inspection procedures.

During machine installation, always check the oil gauges of each tank and replenish oils according to Chapter D.

Additionally, all bearings are pre-lubricated with sealed lubricating grease and require no replenishment.

Cutting fluids must be selected and replenished by the user.

Article body:
Before operating the machine, the following items must be verified.

Refer to the [Operation Manual] for specific procedures.

1）Thoroughly inspect the mechanical and electrical components of the machine to ensure integrity, and verify that the power supply wiring is correctly connected.

2）Power Supply Voltage

Measure the voltage between each phase line (L1, L2, L3) to confirm fluctuations remain within ±10% of the rated value.

3）Power Indication

Activate the [Main Switch] in the power cabinet and verify that the [Power Indicator Light] illuminates.

4）Operational Readiness

Reset the [Emergency Stop Switch], press the [Operational Preparation Switch], and confirm that the [Operational Readiness Completion Indicator] lights up.

5）Do not touch any keys on the MDI panel until the CNC display screen shows the position or alarm interface. Premature interaction may trigger unintended machine movements.
Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation & Installation

Article body:
Confirm the rotation/movement direction of each motor by following these steps.

If direction is reversed, swap any two power cables.

(1) Hydraulic Unit Motor

•    Activate [Operation Preparation Switch] and verify motor rotation direction.

(2) Cutting Fluid Pump

•    Engage [Cutting Fluid Switch] to initiate fluid discharge.
•    Confirm proper rotation direction of the cutting fluid pump during operation.

(3) Chip Conveyor

•    Press [Chip Conveyor Forward Switch] to activate material discharge direction.
Subcategory: Section C Machine Tool Transportation & Installation

Article body:
3.1    Before Powering On 

•    If any inspection results are unsatisfactory, do not use the machine tool until repairs or fixes have been completed.  
•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

•    Please clean up any chips in a timely manner. 
•    Failure to properly manage chips may lead to malfunctions and poor machining results.


3.1.1 Inspect the area around the machine tool for abnormalities. 

1) Check if external piping and hoses are detached. 
2) Inspect the power cables for any broken wires or damage to the outer insulation.  
3) Check for any damage to hydraulic or pneumatic hoses. 
4) Ensure that all doors on the machine tool are properly closed.

3.1.2 Inspect the floor around the machine tool.  

1) Check for any oil leaks: 
•    Cutting fluid, lubricating oil, hydraulic oil, etc.  

2) Clear any obstacles from the work area.
•    Tools, fixtures, measuring instruments, materials, etc.    

3) Clean up any chips that have fallen onto the floor.

3.1.3 Check that the total pressure gauge and two hydraulic operation pressure gauges on the hydraulic station show the correct readings.

1. Hydraulic Station Total Pressure
2. Sub-Spindle Pressure
3. Main Spindle Pressure

3.2    Inspection After Operation Preparation

•    If any inspection results are unsatisfactory, do not operate the machine tool until repairs or fixes have been completed.  
•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

(1) Confirm that the hydraulic unit pump operates with a normal sound. 

(2) Verify the pressure of each hydraulic circuit.  
The total pressure of the hydraulic unit should be 4.0 MPa. 

Check the pressure for the chuck pressure cylinders:   

For 8-inch chucks, pressure should be less than 26 Kgf, For 6-inch chucks, pressure should be less than 25 Kgf.

(3) Confirm the supply pressure of the pneumatic unit.
The standard setting is 0.5 MPa.    

4) Confirm that the lubrication oil pump is functioning correctly. 

5) Check that the air conditioning unit on the electrical cabinet side is operating normally. 

6) Confirm that the switches and indicator lights on the control panel are functioning correctly. 

7) Check for any alarm indications on the system display screen.

•    If any inspection results are unsatisfactory, do not operate the machine tool until repairs or fixes have been completed. 
•    Failure to comply may cause workpieces or chuck jaws to be ejected, potentially leading to personal injury and machine damage.  

(1) Confirm the installation of all tool holders.

(2) Confirm the installation of the turning tools and boring bars. 

(3) Verify the condition of the cutting edges and wear of the turning tools and boring bars. 

(4) Confirm the installation of the chuck jaws. 

(5) Check for any damage to the dust scraper or sliding protective covers.

To minimize the impact of heat generated during the initial automatic operation on workpiece machining accuracy, preheating of the spindle and all moving axes should be performed before automatic operation. 

When preparing the preheating program, in addition to spindle operation (1000 rpm) and rapid feed movements, please include a program that covers various functions. 

•    Caution If the machine has been idle for an extended period (over one week), suddenly running the spindle at high speeds could damage the spindle's oil film, lead-ing to bearing wear.

•    In such cases, set the spindle speed to 300 rpm for about 30 minutes, then increase to 500 rpm for another 30 minutes for preheating.

•    For detailed information about preheating operations, please contact the manufacturer.

•    During Automatic Operation Warning  
•    If any inspection results are unsatisfactory, immediately stop the machine tool.
•    Do not operate the machine until repairs or fixes have been completed. 
•    Failure to comply may cause injury during maintenance or machine damage.

(1) Confirm the operational sound and vibration of the spindle during operation. 

(2） Confirm the sound of the spindle motor during operation.   

(3）Confirm the vibration during automatic operation. 

(4）Monitor the load condition of the spindle motor (data can be observed on the system display screen).  
(5）Confirm the machining accuracy of the workpiece.

•    Do not use compressed air to clean the inside of the machine tool. 
•    Flying chips may enter unintended areas such as guideways, which can lead to machine malfunctions.  

(1) Use a brush and cloth to clean the interior of the machine tool. 


(2) Remove chips from the base and chip conveyor, disposing of them outside the machine, and transport chips from the chip cart to the designated disposal area.

This section outlines the key points for regular maintenance and adjustments to maintain the machine tool's accuracy and longevity. 

Maintenance personnel should also refer to other manuals for specific maintenance procedures. 

For maintenance guidelines not covered in this manual, please refer to the user manuals provided by the manufacturers of commercially available products.

•    When performing maintenance work, always disconnect the power supply before starting.  Contact with high-voltage electricity can be fatal.

•    Thoroughly read and understand the [Safety Guidelines] before operation.
•    Failure to understand the content thoroughly before use can result in personal injury, machine malfunctions, or damage.
•    Maintenance personnel must fully understand and strictly adhere to the precautions for maintenance tasks. 
•    Failure to do so may lead to unforeseen injuries.

The primary objective of maintenance inspections is to detect any abnormalities in the machine early, preventing potential failures, thereby increasing machine uptime and productivity. 

To achieve this, it is essential to create a maintenance schedule based on the [Regular Inspection Checklist], and for maintenance personnel to perform inspections and maintenance according to the schedule. 

Additionally, it is important to consider feedback from daily inspectors and make any necessary adjustments. 

Besides this manual, maintenance should also refer to the operation manuals, electrical diagrams, and ladder diagrams.

To conduct maintenance effectively, ensure the following preparations are made.

By properly preparing, even emergency repairs can be completed quickly. 

Consult with the operator responsible for the machine to understand any abnormal conditions.  

Investigate the situation, plan the scope of work, and establish the sequence of tasks. 

Familiarize yourself with the specifications, structure, and functionality of the maintenance points in advance. 

If working with other departments, ensure thorough discussions and coordination beforehand.   

If parts or consumables need to be replaced, make sure to have them prepared in advance.   
Prepare necessary tools, ladders, and step stools for the work.

7.1    Heading 

If the display shows “APC,” it indicates that the absolute pulse encoder battery has reached the end of its life. 

Please refer to the maintenance manual issued by FANUC for replacing the alkaline dry cell battery. 
 The location of the absolute pulse encoder battery can be found in [Figure A8 - Component Names]. 

7.2    Heading 

If the display shows “BAT,” the CNC unit’s battery (lithium battery) has reached the end of its life. 
Please refer to the FANUC reference disc (debugging and maintenance manual) for instructions on replacing the lithium battery. 

7.3    Heading 

The CNC unit battery is located on the back of the NC device (refer to the reference disc: debugging and maintenance manual).

This section provides a brief description of the machine's intended purpose, operating methods, and key features.

•    Use the machine strictly in accordance with its designated purpose and operating methods.
•    Failure to comply may result in personal injury, machine malfunction, or damage.
•    Thoroughly read and fully understand the [Safety Guidelines] before operation.
•    Improper use due to insufficient understanding may lead to personal injury, machine malfunction, or damage.

2.1         Basic Precautions for Machine Use

Thoroughly read the machine manual and all safety labels affixed to the machine

▲    Before installation, operation, maintenance, or repair, carefully study this manual to fully understand operational procedures and potential hazards that may cause personal in-jury or machine damage.
▲    Safety labels on the machine indicate potential danger zones. Read and comprehend all warnings completely.

Machine Application Scope

▲    This machine is a CNC horizontal lathe designed exclusively for metal cutting. Do not use it for purposes beyond its original design specifications.
▲    Perform machining operations only within the machine's rated working envelope. 
▲    Always use the standard fixtures provided with the machine. 
▲    If using third-party fixtures/jigs, ensure spindle speeds remain within the fixture's rated limits and verify proper workpiece clamping before operation. Our company assumes no liability for injuries or damage caused by improper fixture usage.

Strict Compliance with Workshop Safety Regulations

▲    Operate the machine strictly in accordance with workshop safety protocols.
▲    Normally operate the machine individually. For multi-person operations, all personnel must confirm operational procedures and sequence, executing commands under uni-fied supervision.
▲    Never operate the machine when fatigued, emotionally distressed, under medication influence, or after alcohol consumption, as impaired judgment may cause operational errors leading to injury or equipment damage.

Proper Safety Attire

▲    Operators must wear dedicated work uniforms, safety shoes, protective headgear, and safety goggles.
▲    Prohibit gloves, exposed neckties, scarves, or any loose/dangling accessories that may become entangled in moving machine components, potentially causing severe injury.

Maintain Safe Working Environment

▲    Keep the machine perimeter clean and unobstructed. Immediately clean any spilled lubricants or coolant to prevent slip hazards.
▲    Maintain adequate clearance space around the machine.
▲    Never place tools, parts, or objects on machine covers, doors, slides, or any location that may interfere with normal operation.

Fire Prevention Measures

▲    Exercise extreme caution as machine lubricants and coolants are flammable.
▲    Ensure proper fire prevention/extinguishing equipment is available near the machine.
▲    Keep flammable materials and high-temperature devices away from the machine.

Unauthorized Modifications Prohibited

▲    Any machine modification requires our company's prior written authorization.
▲    We assume no responsibility for injuries or damage resulting from unauthorized struc-tural modifications or operations beyond approved specifications.

Electrical & Maintenance Safety

▲    Verify proper electrical grounding per GB5226.1-2002 standards before operation.
▲    Never touch controls or switches with wet hands.
▲    High-voltage components (electrical cabinets, transformers, motors, etc.) may only be serviced by qualified personnel to prevent personal injury or equipment damage.
▲    Do not connect equipment causing electrical interference (e.g., welders, induction heaters) to the power distribution panel.
▲    Clearly mark "MACHINE FAULT - DO NOT OPERATE" during maintenance downtime.
▲    Only qualified technicians may perform maintenance after disconnecting all power sources and setting switches to [OFF] position.

2.2    Precautions During Machine Operation

Precautions for First-Time Use of the Machine

▲    This machine is equipped with multiple safety devices to protect both the operator and the equipment. However, do not rely solely on these safety features and neglect opera-tional safety regulations, as failure or malfunction of these devices may pose risks to personnel or the machine.  Therefore, before operating the machine, thoroughly review this manual and ensure the operator has received professional training and is capable of independent operation.
▲    For transportation safety, the protective doors are secured. The user must remove these fixtures before operating the machine; otherwise, machine damage may occur.
▲    Before initial startup, clean off all anti-rust oil from the machine using kerosene and lubricate each lubrication point as specified. Do not use deteriorated or contaminated lubricants.

Verification of Safety Device Functionality

▲     Before operating the machine, confirm that all safety devices are functioning properly. If any safety device is faulty, the machine must not be used until repairs are completed.
▲    Before machining, check whether the X/Z-axis soft limits and mechanical limit settings are correct.
▲    Verify that the "Emergency Stop Switch" is effective and reliable.

Precautions During Machine Operation

▲    Do not wear gloves while operating the machine.
▲    Do not open the sliding door during machine operation.
▲    Do not touch moving parts of the machine with hands or any tools.
▲    If long bar stock extends beyond the rear of the spindle, install warning signs or protec-tive railings to prevent injury.
▲    If abnormal noise or vibration occurs, immediately stop the machine for inspection. Re-sume operation only after confirming normal conditions.
▲    Memorize the location of the "Emergency Stop Switch" to ensure it can be pressed im-mediately in emergencies, preventing hazards.
▲    Remove chips promptly.
▲    When cleaning the machining area inside the machine, first turn off the power and use cleaning tools. Chips generated during cutting are typically high-temperature and sharp—never remove them by hand to avoid injury.
▲    Ensure proper machine organization, maintenance, and cleaning.
▲    CNC and PLC parameters are preset at the factory and generally do not require modifi-cation.
▲     If changes are necessary, ensure a thorough understanding of parameter functions and
▲    make adjustments under the guidance of our technical personnel.
▲    Do not modify machine technical parameters beyond allowable limits (e.g., maximum spindle speed, rapid tool movement speed, etc.). Our company is not responsible for equipment damage or accidents caused by unauthorized modifications.

2.3    Waste Disposal

▲    Used lubricants and cutting fluids must not be discharged directly onto the ground or into drains.  Dispose of waste liquids properly at designated environmental disposal sites.
▲    Upon final scrapping of the machine, non-recyclable components (e.g., electrical parts, rubber components, lubricants) that are toxic, hazardous, or non-degradable must be processed in accordance with relevant regulations.

Operating Instructions

Before machining on the lathe, ensure the following elements are confirmed:
(1) Workpiece clamping method
(2) Appropriate cutting tools meeting process requirements
(3) Machining parameters and programmed procedures compliant with process specifications
The following details the specific operating methods for the L4 series, including optional functions:

4.1    Workpiece Clamping Methods

4.1.1 This machine adopts a hollow hydraulic chuck for workpiece clamping.

4.1.2 The main spindle is equipped with a 10-inch hydraulic chuck, while the sub-spindle has an 8-inch hydraulic chuck.

4.1.3 During assembly, ensure the draw tube is fully threaded into the hydraulic cylinder and the chuck is properly connected to the draw tube. Failure to do so may cause machine dam-age.

4.1.4 The system pressure of the hydraulic station can be adjusted via the regulating screw on the hydraulic pump (typically set to 40 kgf). Cylinder pressure is adjusted by rotating the pres-sure-reducing valve’s knob. Maximum allowable pressure for 10-inch hollow power chucks: ≤32 kgf Maximum allowable pressure for 8-inch hollow power chucks: ≤26 kgf.

4.1.5 The chuck features a through-hole design for bar stock feed-through machining.

4.1.6 For long bar stock machining, this machine is often paired with a bar feeder.

4.1.7 Optional accessories include solid hydraulic chucks, pneumatic chucks, collet chucks, or custom special chucks per customer requirements.

★    Unauthorized chuck modifications are strictly prohibited. The company assumes no re-sponsibility for machine damage or safety incidents caused by such alterations.

4.2    Workpiece Support Methods

This machine features dual-spindle/dual-chuck functionality. Workpiece support methods are as follows:
4.2.1 For long shaft machining: Both hydraulic chucks simultaneously clamp the workpiece ends with synchronized spindle rotation to ensure precision.

4.2.2 The W-axis of the sub-spindle can achieve servo tailstock functionality when used with matching fixtures/centers (consult fixture manufacturers or our company for details).

4.2.3 Long shaft machining may incorporate a bar feeder for synchronized operation For single-chuck machines with tailstock (hydraulic/servo), workpiece support methods are as follows:

4.3    Workpiece Loading/Unloading Methods

Two modes are available: manual and automatic.

4.3.1 Manual Operation
Directly load/unload workpieces to/from the chuck by hand.

4.3.2 Gantry/Robotic Arm Handling
Automated pickup of raw workpieces from trays to the chuck, or finished part removal to trays.
Enables continuous production lines for extended unmanned operation, improving efficien-cy/output while reducing labor.

4.3.3 Bar Feeder Method
Automated bar stock feeding through the chuck’s draw tube hole (left side of lathe).
Processes repeat cyclically: machining → cutting → blank feeding.

4.4    Chip Disposal Methods

To discharge chips outside the machine tool and remove them from around the workpiece and cutting tool, the following methods are available.

4.4.1 Manual Chip Disposal
A manual chip collection box is installed inside the machine tool’s body cavity. During chip removal, chips are manually raked out of the machine using tools.

4.4.2 Conveyor Belt Method
This machine can be optionally equipped with a chain-type automatic chip conveyor. The con-veyor is placed inside the coolant tank and moves with it. Chips are discharged from the right side of the machine. During machining, chips falling onto the conveyor are automatically ex-pelled outside the machine and collected in a chip cart placed below the conveyor’s dis-charge port.

4.4.3 Chip Removal at the Machining Area
For chip removal at the machining area and cooling of the tool tip, coolant is typically sprayed through nozzles on the tool turret. In special cases, high-pressure air blasting may also be used.

4.4.4 High-Pressure Chip Flushing
For materials that are difficult to break into chips or during deep-hole machining, an optional high-pressure cutting oil pump can be used to spray cutting oil for chip removal.

4.5    Cutting Fluid Application Methods

During cutting, the tool temperature rises and undergoes high-intensity pressure. To reduce cutting resistance, control built-up edge, cool the tool, improve workpiece surface finish, en-hance dimensional accuracy, extend tool life, and remove chips, an appropriate cutting fluid must be used.

4.5.1 Cutting Fluid Spray Method
This machine is equipped with an independent coolant tank beneath the bed. The coolant pump delivers cutting fluid from the tank to the cutting point through spray nozzles on the power tool turret for cooling and chip flushing during machining.

4.5.2 Cutting Fluid Storage
The cutting fluid is stored in the independent coolant tank beneath the bed, with a capacity of approximately 200 liters. Using the machine with sufficient cutting fluid prevents temperature rise and ensures stable machining accuracy.

4.5.3 Cutting Fluid Selection
Users should select the appropriate cutting fluid based on the workpiece material and ma-chining requirements. Improper selection may affect tool life and part machining accuracy.

4.5.4 Coolant Tank Filter Maintenance
The coolant tank filter must be cleaned regularly to ensure an adequate and smooth flow of cutting fluid through the coolant pipes.

4.5.6 Coolant Pump Wiring Verification
When connecting the main power supply, the coolant pump must be detached from the cool-ant tank and activated. Verify the impeller rotation direction using the arrow indicator on the pump. If the direction is incorrect, rewire until the impeller rotates correctly.  Failure to do so will affect the pump’s output and service life.

4.6    Optional Special Accessories

4.6.1 Automatic Tool Presetter
This machine can be optionally equipped with a precision automatic tool presetter. Under program command, the presetter automatically detects the tool tip position, enabling quick setup of the workpiece’s initial machining position in the control system. Additionally, after prolonged tool use, the presetter can recheck the tool tip position to compensate for tool wear and maintain part machining accuracy.

4.6.2 Bar Feeder
The connection and operation of the bar feeder should follow the electrical circuit diagram and the bar feeder’s instruction manual.

4.6.3 In-Process Workpiece Inspection System
This machine can be optionally equipped with an in-process workpiece inspection system. The wireless inspection receiver is mounted above the spindle, while the contact-type probe is in-stalled on the boring tool holder of the turret. After machining, the turret can be program-controlled to move the probe close to and in contact with the workpiece. The system detects deviations and automatically compensates them in the program to ensure machining accuracy.

4.6.5 High-Pressure Coolant Pump System
Users can opt for a high-pressure coolant pump system based on machining requirements. It aids in chip breaking and tooltip cooling during part machining, effectively extending tool life and ensuring better compatibility with automatic loading/unloading mechanisms.

•    Install the machine under appropriate environmental conditions.
•    Non-compliance may lead to electrical system failures in the machine or peripheral equipment, causing operational issues.

1.1    Installation Environment 

The NC lathe is equipped with precision components.  To maximize performance and longevi-ty, ensure proper environmental conditions.

Indoor location: Protected from weather, with controlled temperature, dust, vibration, and noise levels.

Ambient Temperature
•    Room temp: 0–35°C
•    Rate of Change: ≤1.1°C/min
•    Humidity: ≤75% (at 20°C)
•    Optimal Temperature Range: 17–26°C
•    (24-hour variation within ±2°C)
•    Vibration: ≤0.5 G

Environmental Considerations
1）    Do not install the machine outdoors. Additionally, avoid locations exposed to direct sunlight or near air conditioning vents. Significant temperature fluctuations will directly affect machining accuracy.
2）    Do not install the machine in areas with excessive vibration or noise from surrounding equipment. This may lead to machine malfunctions and adversely affect machining accuracy.
3）    Avoid humid environments (above 76% humidity), as well as areas with debris, dust, or mist. This may lead to machine malfunctions and
4）    Do not place unrelated items near the machine that may interfere with its operation.

1.2    Foundation

To ensure optimal machining accuracy, the machine must be installed on a solid foundation.
The foundation should be constructed according to standard methods for machine tool instal-lation, considering the ground conditions of the factory site. [Reference: Foundation Drawing] 
Foundation Construction Considerations
1）    Typically, the concrete floor thickness should be ≥400 mm.
2）    The foundation area should extend ~300 mm around the machine base.
3）    When selecting the installation site, account for: Control cabinet door clearance, Chip conveyor, Cutting oil tank, and Maintenance space.

1.3    L4 Foundation Drawing

•    Only operators certified in forklift, crane, or rigging operations may handle the ma-chine.
•    Untrained personnel may cause injuries or damage to the machine.
•    Do not use frayed or bent cables for lifting.
•    Improper rigging may lead to machine drops, risking injury or damage.

2.1    Forklift Transportation

2.1.1 This machine features an integrated base structure with forklift pockets for transport.
2.1.2 Clear obstacles and restrict unauthorized personnel during transport. Operate the fork-lift at low speed—sudden acceleration, braking, or turns may tip the machine or forklift.
2.1.3 Remove the decorative panel at the position shown below.

2.1.4 Use a 10-ton or larger forklift, aligning the forks as shown in the diagram.

2.1.5 After positioning the machine, install the provided leveling screws before lowering it.
Place base pads beneath the screws, ensuring the recess aligns with the screw. Adjust the screws to level the machine after placement.

2.2    Using a Crane

Moving the machine tool using a crane is highly hazardous and requires full attention.

2.2.1 Caution: Key Points for Lifting Operation


(1) Remove the left and right side covers and install dedicated lifting bars on the machine tool.
(2) Use lifting cables with a diameter of at least 16 mm.
The machine weight is approximately 4,400 kg.
(3) Prepare I-beam lifting tools (approx. 320 mm × 150 mm).
(4) The machine’s center of gravity is indicated in the lifting diagram.
If unbalanced, adjust using a chain hoist.
(5) Lift slowly—after the cables are taut, recheck the lifting setup. Before fully raising the ma-chine, reconfirm the lifting position and center of gravity.
Lower slowly and verify the placement position before final contact with the ground.
After installation, remove the internal fixing tools (yellow) for the saddle and cross slide.

2.2.2 Fabrication of Lifting Fixtures for Machine Tool Mounting Holes


(1) Left-side fixture (2 pcs)

(2) Right-side fixture (2 pcs)

2.2.3 Machine Tool Lifting Diagram

Proper leveling is critical—it directly affects machining accuracy and machine lifespan.

Before leveling: Remove internal fixing tools (yellow). Clean off anti-rust grease from the X-axis and Z-axis sliding surfaces.

Leveling procedure:
(1) Place a pre-calibrated level (sensitivity: 0.02 mm/m) on the base sliding surface, aligned with the Z-axis and X-axis directions.
(2) Adjust the leveling screws at the base bottom until the bubble centers.

•    Thoroughly read the [Safety Guidelines] and fully understand the content before operation.

•    Thoroughly study the [Operation Manual] and [Programming Manual] to master proper operating procedures.

To ensure optimal machine tool performance, operators must fully understand its functions and capabilities for proper utilization. Routine lubrication and inspection by operators are essential to maintain machining accuracy and prolong equipment service life.

Perform inspections according to the daily checklist.

Report any abnormalities discovered during inspection to maintenance supervisors immediately.

Maintenance personnel shall conduct servicing. Contact the manufacturer if issues cannot be resolved locally.

•    Never allow oil spills on floors or work areas.
•    Clean spills immediately to prevent slip hazards

(1) Store oil containers indoors, protected from rain and direct sunlight.
(2) Tighten drum lids securely to prevent contamination by dust/water.

2.2    Oil Refill Precautions

(1) Use dedicated oil dispensers only. Avoid cross-contamination.
(2) Never remove filter screens from oil tank fill ports.
(3) Prohibited: Degraded/expired oils or oils containing foreign matter.
(4) Never mix oils from different manufacturers.
(5) If non-specified or mixed oils are used, flush the entire oil tank and piping system immediately.

2.3    Waste Oil Disposal Precautions

(1) Never dispose of waste oil improperly.
(2) Contract licensed waste oil processing providers for disposal.

2.5    Cutting Fluids

Cutting fluids are classified into two types: non-water-soluble cutting fluids and water-soluble cutting fluids.


Their performance is evaluated based on: Primary performance (cutting performance enhancement): Relates to cutting efficiency, tool life, and workpiece surface accuracy. Secondary performance (ease of use): Relates to fluid stability, safety, and cost-effectiveness.

Improper selection of cutting fluids may lead to machine tool malfunctions. Consider the following criteria when selecting cutting fluids:

(1) Primary Performance (Cutting Performance Enhancement)

•    Reduces cutting resistance, eliminates built-up edge (BUE), and improves surface fin-ish.
•    Lowers cutting temperature and extends tool life.

(2) Secondary Performance (Ease of Use)

•    Prevents rust on workpieces and machine components.
•    Minimizes smoke and mist generation.
•    Ensures consistent quality (no separation, layering, or degradation).
•    Non-toxic and safe for operators (including low odor).
•    Non-corrosive to paint coatings.
User Responsibility: Supply the designated cutting fluid. Monitor the fluid level in the tank and refill as needed, typically to ¾ of the tank height.

•    Do not operate the machine until all identified issues are fully repaired.
•    Failure to comply may cause workpiece/chuck jaw ejection, leading to personal injury or machine damage.

1）    Verify proper installation of all tool holders.
2）    Inspect mounting of turning tools and boring bars.
3）    Examine insert wear conditions on turning/boring tools.
4）    Confirm chuck jaw installation integrity.
5）    Check for damage on wipers or sliding covers.

To minimize thermal effects on machining accuracy during initial automatic operation, execute spindle and axis warm-up cycles before production.

When programming warm-up routines: Include spindle rotation (1000 min⁻¹) and rapid feed movements Incorporate comprehensive function testing.

•    After prolonged machine inactivity (>1 week), abrupt high-speed spindle rotation may break down lubricant films, causing bearing seizure.

1）    Run spindle at 300 rpm for 30 minutes
2）    Increase to 500 rpm for 30 minutes
3）    Contact our technical department for detailed warm-up protocols

•    Do not use compressed air to clean the machine interior.
•    Scattered chips may enter unexpected areas (e.g., slideways), potentially causing machine malfunctions.

1）    Clean the machine interior using brushes and rags.

2）    Remove chips from the base and chip conveyor, and transport waste chips in the chip cart to the designated area.

Article title: Machine Tool Manual L4 - User Notice
Article body:
Before operating this machine, carefully read the contents of this manual to ensure proper op-eration.  If you have any questions, please contact the nearest dealer or our company.

Keep this manual near the machine for easy reference.

Strictly adhere to the safety precautions in this manual and the warning labels on the machine.  Violations may result in serious personal injury or damage to the machine.

Note: Some illustrations in this manual depict detailed explanations by showing the machine with covers or doors removed. For safe operation, all protective covers must be properly in-stalled.

Comply with all applicable laws and regulations in the country or region where the machine is used.

This machine is intended for use only by personnel with fundamental knowledge of machine tool operations.

Our company assumes no responsibility for accidents caused by unauthorized operation, maintenance, or programming by untrained personnel or those without sufficient knowledge of machine tools.  When replacing parts, use only those specified by our company.

Our company bears no responsibility for accidents resulting from the use of non-specified parts.  The use of unauthorized parts may not only degrade machine performance but also compromise safety, potentially leading to personal injury or machine damage.

Although our company does not specify cutting fluids, consult thoroughly with cutting fluid manufacturers and exercise caution in selection. We assume no responsibility for accidents related to cutting fluids.

All rights, including copyrights, for this manual belong to our company.  The drawings and technical specifications contained herein may not be disclosed, reproduced, translated, or copied into any electronic device or medium without our prior consent.

We reserve the right to modify the contents of this manual without prior notice.
Company names and product names mentioned in this manual are generally trademarks or registered trademarks of their respective owners.

The L4 Dual-Spindle CNC Lathe is a high-performance machine independently developed by our company. Its primary function involves mounting metal workpieces on the spindle, where the spindle servo motor rotates the workpiece while the NC (Numerical Control) system coordinates tool post movement to perform efficient turning and milling operations.

Through synchronized movements of the main spindle, sub-spindle, X-axis, Z-axis, and Y-axis, this machine completes complex precision machining including turning, milling, threading, and drilling - in a single setup.

Featuring modular design, it can be configured into different product variations based on specific requirements.

3.1    Main Structure of the Machine Tool

The L4 dual-spindle CNC lathe adopts a 30° inclined guideway integrated bed structure. The bed is designed through finite element analysis (FEA) with rationally distributed rib plates, ensuring high rigidity and thermal stability. The oversized X/Z-axis moving saddles drive the servo turret for longitudinal and axial cutting operations. The main spindle is a 15 kW belt-driven mechanical spindle with a built-in housing design, featuring high-rigidity spindle bearings to en-sure stable cutting performance and minimal thermal deformation. Opposite the main spindle is an A2-6 servo-driven sub-spindle, which adopts an 18.8 kW direct-drive motorized spindle structure. After the first operation on the main spindle, the sub-spindle moves to the workpiece via servo motor drive, clamps the workpiece, and transports it to the designated area for reverse-side machining. This machine offers advantages such as high rigidity, flexible machining capability, large working area, reliable protection, efficient chip removal, and user-friendly operation. It employs modular structural design technology with full-enclosure protection, ensuring a compact and aesthetically pleasing appearance.

3.2    Key Features of the Machine Tool

3.2.1 The 30° inclined guideway integrated bed is cast from high-quality HT300 gray cast iron, optimized via FEA with well-arranged rib plates for superior rigidity and thermal stability.  The side chip removal system provides large-capacity chip handling, and the coolant tank and chip conveyor can be pulled out from the front for flexible workshop layout and easy maintenance.

3.2.2 The large-span saddle, turret housing, and middle saddle are designed as an integrated structure, further enhancing cutting rigidity and stability.

3.2.3 Equipped with a proprietary 12-station BMT65 servo turret and a robust integrated turret housing.

3.2.4 All three axes utilize 45 mm wide linear roller guides, offering high rigidity, long service life, fast movement, excellent precision retention, and low maintenance—ideal for precision machining.

3.2.5 High-precision imported ball screws are employed on all axes, ensuring fast, stable, and accurate motion. The screws feature double-end pre-tensioned support and specialized im-ported bearings. Precision-calculated preload assembly minimizes machining errors caused by thermal expansion.

3.2.6 The main spindle adopts a high-rigidity A2-8 design with double-row roller bearings, paired with a 10-inch hydraulic chuck for minimal thermal deformation and superior cutting performance.

3.2.7 The subspindle is an A2-6 motorized spindle with an 8-inch hydraulic chuck, delivering 18.8 kW power and a maximum speed of 4,500 RPM.  Its forced oil-cooling circulation ensures smooth, low-vibration operation. After the first operation on the main spindle, the subspindle synchronizes rotation, clamps the workpiece, and moves it to the right for secondary machin-ing by the powered turret.

3.2.8 Dual lubrication systems: grease lubrication for spindle and screw bearings (long-life, maintenance-free) and automatic centralized oil lubrication for screws, guides, and turret gears (reliable and sufficient).

3.2.9 Energy-efficient LED lighting with extended lifespan; tri-color status indicator for real-time machine state monitoring, facilitating visual management.

3.2.10 Compact design, full-enclosure protection, efficient chip removal, high-strength tem-pered safety glass, bright work lights, and ergonomic operating height ensure safety, comfort, and aesthetics.

The following operating and machining methods are prohibited, as they may lead to personal injury or machine damage.

Additionally, refer to the [Machine Safety Operation Guidelines] for safe usage practices.

•    Operate within the permissible rotation speed limits of the spindle, chuck, and pres-sure cylinder.
•    Non-compliance may cause workpiece ejection, leading to personal injury or machine damage.
•    Keep the front door closed during operation.
•    Do not operate with covers open or moving parts exposed.
•    Non-compliance may result in entanglement with rotating/moving parts, causing injury.
•    Do not remove or modify safety devices, protective covers, or safety windows during operation.
•    Non-compliance may result in entanglement with rotating/moving parts, causing injury.
•    Do not operate the machine when the bar stock protrudes from the rear of the spindle. 
•    Non-compliance may cause the bar to swing or rotate dangerously, resulting in injury or machine damage.

•    Do not mount cutting tools on the spindle side for machining.
•    Example:
•    Prohibit mounting workpieces on the turret and tools on the spindle for milling operations.
•    Non-compliance may cause machine damage.
•    When clamping workpieces on the chuck:
•    Do not feed the turret in the Z- or X-axis while the spindle is stationary for keyway cutting or planning operations.
•    Such operations may exert abnormal thrust on the feed system’s lead screws, causing malfunctions.

•    Do not use compressed air to blow away or clean chips inside the machine.
•    Scattered chips may enter unexpected areas of the inclined structure, leading to machine failure.
•    When machining flammable materials or generating hazardous substances:
•    Consult experts, wear protective gear, and install safety/environmental equipment.
•    Non-compliance may cause fires or personal injury.
•    Operators must receive proper NC lathe training and guidance.
•    Non-compliance may result in injury or machine damage.
•    Maintenance personnel must also undergo NC lathe operation and maintenance train-ing.
•    Additionally, qualified electricians or crane operators should be employed as needed.
•    Non-compliance may result in injury or machine damage.

1.1    Structure of the Spindle

The structure and key dimensions of the spindle are shown in [Figure B1-1: Main Spindle Di-mensions].

The spindle is lubricated with high-performance, long-life Kluber high-speed grease (Germany) pre-applied at the factory. No routine maintenance is required.

1.2    Spindle Motor Rotation Speed / Torque Output Curve

Article body:
5.1    Heading 
The L4 dual-spindle CNC machine tool is equipped with three sets of lead screws, corresponding to the X-axis (workpiece radial movement), Z-axis (workpiece axial movement),     
and W-axis (sub-spindle axial movement).

5.2    Heading
All three lead screws adopt a pre-tensioned structure supported at both ends, enhancing rigidity and reducing thermal elongation.

5.3    Heading
Each lead screw uses specialized angular contact ball bearings with an inner diameter of 30 mm, paired with precisely adjusted pre-tensioning.

Subcategory: Section B Overview and Specifications of Components

Article body:
8.1    Lubrication Classification

This machine employs both grease lubrication and oil lubrication systems.

8.1.1 Grease Lubrication

All bearings use high-speed, long-life grease requiring no maintenance under normal opera-tion.
The bevel gears in the self-developed tool turret center module use high-speed, long-life grease requiring replenishment every 1,000 operating hours or 6 months.

8.1.2 Oil Lubrication

The lubrication of the machine tool's linear guides, ball screw nuts, and power tool turret transmission gears is achieved through a centralized oil supply from the automatic lubrication pump.  When the lubricating oil in the pump's oil tank is insufficient, the system will issue an alarm, at which point the oil tank should be promptly refilled with lubricating oil.

8.1.3  

Thin oil lubricants such as ISO VG 32–46 petroleum-based lubricating oil may be used. The user can set the lubrication cycle of the pump according to requirements.

•    To ensure sufficient lubrication at all lubrication points, our company recommends set-ting the lubrication pump to operate every 30 minutes, with each lubrication cycle last-ing no less than 10 seconds.  If improper user settings result in insufficient lubrication and subsequent machine tool damage, our company shall not be held responsible.

8.1.4  Precautions

•    All lubricating oils must be clean and free from contamination. 
•    Regularly inspect the lubrication pump and replenish lubricating oil in a timely manner. If the oil is found to be contaminated, it must be replaced immediately.

8.2    Lubrication Distribution Diagram (Lubrication Pump Capacity: 3L)

8.3    Lubrication Unit

The lubrication unit adopts a volumetric electric oil pump.

The lubrication interval time is adjustable.

Article title: Section C Machine Tool Transportation and Installation
Article body:
This chapter explains the sequence from machine installation to operation.

•    During collaborative work, ensure mutual coordination and verification.
•    Non-compliance may result in entanglement with rotating/moving parts, causing injury.
•    Thoroughly read the [Safety Guidelines] and fully understand the content before opera-tion. Improper use due to insufficient understanding may cause personal injury or ma-chine damage.
•    Install protective barriers and "No Entry" warning signs in work areas. Non-compliance may endanger nearby personnel.
•    Operators must wear helmets, safety shoes, and appropriate work attire.
•    Failure to do so may result in cuts, impacts, or other injuries.

•    Risk of fatal electric shock—exercise extreme caution during electrical work.
•    electric shock may result in fatal injury.
•    Only licensed electricians should perform electrical installations. electric shock may result in fatal injury.
•    Open the electrical cabinet door at the rear of the machine tool. Route the power cable through the cable entry hole into the electrical cabinet, then connect the wires in the sequence of A1, A2, A3, and PE (grounding).
•    Power capacity and cable specifications vary depending on the machine model.
•    Insert the power cable from the left side of the power distribution cabinet. Verify L1, L2, and L3 phases before connecting to the main circuit breaker.
•    Use grounding wires (PE) with a cross-sectional area of at least 14 mm² (thick copper wire), maintaining grounding resistance below 100 ohms.
•    Do not share power sources with high-capacity equipment such as welding machines or EDM machines.  Voltage fluctuations from such equipment may cause control sys-tem malfunctions.
•    Before wiring, ensure both the machine's short-circuit protection switch and the power supply circuit breaker are in the "OFF" position.

Verify motor rotation/direction using the following sequence. 

If reversed, swap any two of the three power cables

(1) Hydraulic Unit
Check motor rotation after pressing [Operation Preparation Switch].

(2) Cutting Fluid System
Confirm pump rotation direction when activating [Cutting Fluid Switch] and observing fluid dis-charge.

(3) Chip Conveyor
Ensure conveyor moves toward the discharge direction when pressing [Conveyor Forward Switch].

3.1    Before Power-On 

•    Do not operate the machine if any inspection item fails. Repair or resolve the issue before use. 
•    Non-compliance may result in injury or machine damage.

•    Remove chips promptly. 
•    Accumulated chips may cause malfunctions or poor machining results.

3.1.1 Inspect the Machine Surroundings for Abnormalities 

1）    Check for loose/disconnected external piping or tubing. 
2）    Inspect power cables for breaks or insulation damage.
3）    Verify hydraulic/pneumatic hoses for leaks or damage.
4）    Ensure all machine doors are closed. 

3.1.2 Inspect the Floor Around the Machine 

1）    Check for fluid leaks:
•    Cutting fluid, lubricants, hydraulic oil, etc. 

2）    Remove obstacles and organize the workspace:
•    - Tools, fixtures, measuring instruments, raw materials, etc. 

3）    Clear fallen chips from the floor. 

3.1.3 Verify Hydraulic Pressure Gauges

Ensure the main hydraulic station pressure gauge and two auxiliary hydraulic operation pressure gauges display correct values.

3.2    Post-Startup Preparation Checks 

•    Do not operate the machine until all identified issues are fully repaired.
•    Non-compliance may result in injury or machine damage.

1）    Verify that the hydraulic unit pump operates with normal sound.

2）    Check pressure levels in all hydraulic circuits:
•    Main hydraulic unit pressure: 4.0 MPa
•    Chuck pressure cylinder suitability: 8-inch chuck: ≤26 kgf6-inch chuck: ≤25 kgf

3）    Confirm pneumatic unit supply pressure:
•    0.5 MPa (standard setting)

4）    Inspect proper operation of the lubrication pump.

5）    Ensure normal functioning of the electrical cabinet side-panel air conditioner.

6）    Test all operator panel switches and indicator lights.

7）    Check the system display for alarm messages.

•    Immediately stop the machine if abnormalities are detected.
•    Do not resume operation until complete repairs are made.
•    Non-compliance may result in injury or machine damage.

1）    Monitor spindle noise and vibration during operation.

2）    Check drive motor acoustics.

3）    Observe machine vibrations during cycles.

4）    Monitor spindle motor load (displayed on system screen).

5）    Verify workpiece machining accuracy.

Maintenance tasks involve inherent hazards;  strict adherence to the following protocols is mandatory:

•    EPower must be locked out at the main switch before servicing electrical components.
•    Electrocution or entanglement in rotating/moving parts if powered on.
•    Display "DO NOT POWER ON" warning signs during maintenance.
•    Only authorized personnel with electrical safety training may perform electrical system maintenance.
•    Verify actions with coworkers through clear communication during collaborative tasks.
•    Always confirm mutual acknowledgment during joint operations.
•    Failure to obtain confirmation before operation may result in personal injuries such as electric shock, entanglement in rotating parts, or crushing by moving components.
•    Always disconnect power before entering the machine for maintenance.
•    Performing inspection or maintenance inside the machine while powered on may lead to unexpected machine movement, causing injuries from entanglement in rotating parts or crushing by moving components.

•    Wear appropriate protective equipment and work clothing.
•    Otherwise, injuries such as cuts, impacts, or entanglement in rotating parts may occur.
•    Display a "MAINTENANCE IN PROGRESS" sign during maintenance to alert others.
•    Failure to do so may result in accidental machine operation by other personnel, leading to injuries.
•    Use tools suitable for the maintenance task.
•    Improper tools may cause personal injury or damage to the machine.
•    Use a crane when handling heavy objects.
•    Otherwise, loss of balance during work may lead to crushing injuries from falling ob-jects. Do not use frayed or knotted cables, as they may cause dropped loads and injuries.

This section outlines periodic maintenance procedures to preserve machine accuracy and lifespan.

Note: Maintenance personnel must also refer to other manuals for additional guidelines.

This manual does not cover maintenance procedures for commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) components. 

Always follow the manufacturer’s instructions.

•    Always disconnect power before maintenance.  Contact with high voltage may result in fatal injury.

•    Thoroughly read the [Safety Guidelines] and fully understand the content before opera-tion.
•    Failure to comply may lead to personal injury or machine damage.
•    Maintenance personnel must strictly adhere to safety precautions.
•    Negligence may cause unexpected accidents. Consult the manufacturer's manuals.

Article title: 1 The primary objectives of maintenance inspections
Article body:
It is to identify machine abnormalities at an early stage, prevent potential failures, and enhance operational rates and productivity. To achieve this, it is critical to: Develop a scheduled plan based on the [Periodic Inspection Checklist] for systematic execution by maintenance personnel.

Address feedback from daily operators and take appropriate corrective actions.

Refer to additional documentation such as the Operation Manual, Circuit Diagrams, and Ladder Diagrams for comprehensive maintenance guidance.

To perform maintenance efficiently, prepare the following in advance. 

Proper preparation ensures quicker repairs during emergencies.

Obtain details of abnormalities from the machine operator to assess the situation.

Investigate the issue and plan the scope and sequence of work.

Familiarize yourself with the specifications, structure, and functions of the maintenance points in advance.

If coordination with other departments is required, discuss thoroughly with relevant personnel beforehand.

Prepare replacement parts and consumables in advance if needed.

Ensure all necessary tools, ladders, platforms, and other work equipment are ready.

The belt transmits the spindle motor's output to the spindle accurately.

Since belt tension affects cutting efficiency and lifespan, setting the correct tension is critical.

Over prolonged operation, belts lose tension due to elongation and wear, requiring regular inspection and adjustment.

Adjustment Method: Loosen the 4 mounting screws on the motor baseplate. Adjust the belt tension using the adjustment bolt.

Tension Measurement Methods: UNITTA Sonic Tension Meter

BANDO Tension Gauge (see diagram below for measurement procedure)

1.1 Personal Protection Warnings

Warning: This machine tool is equipped with relevant warning labels in areas that may pose a risk of personal injury. These labels serve as warnings to the operator. Therefore, under no circumstances should the warning labels be removed. Operators must pay attention to the content of the warnings to ensure personal protection and avoid injury.

1.2 Safety Device Warnings

Warning: Safety devices do not guarantee absolute safety in the event of accidents or hazardous conditions. When using this machine tool, always adhere to the relevant precautions.

1.2.1 Installation Location of Safety Devices

The installation locations of the safety devices are shown in the diagram below.

Machine Tool Safety Device Locations

 

Functional Description of Safety Devices

  • Door Interlock Switch (Optional)

The machine cannot operate in automatic mode unless the sliding door is fully closed. If the sliding door is opened during automatic operation, the machine will switch to a feed-hold state.
In manual mode, operations such as spindle collet clamping, spindle rotation, and coolant control can still be performed without closing the door, facilitating part adjustment.

  • Main Circuit Breaker

If excessive current is detected in the machine, the power supply will be automatically cut off.

  • Emergency Stop Button

A red emergency stop button is located on the machine's control panel. In emergencies such as fire, earthquake, or lightning, or when evacuation is necessary, press this button to stop the machine before leaving.

Note: Pressing the emergency stop button during machine operation may damage the workpiece, cutting tools, and the machine itself.

To reset the emergency stop state, ensure safety and then rotate the emergency stop button clockwise.

Precautions Before Operation

  1. Before starting the machine, the operator must understand the following:
    • Location of the emergency stop button and safety devices
    • Content of all warning labels
    • Method to stop the machine in an emergency
    • Functions, operation methods, and sequence of switches and buttons on the control panel
    • Correct startup and shutdown procedures
    • Handling methods for abnormal situations
    • Method to cut off the main circuit breaker
  2. Operators must not operate the machine under conditions that may compromise personal safety or the safe operation of the machine, such as after consuming alcohol, excessive fatigue, or poor health.
  3. Operators must wear appropriate work attire (avoid loose or oversized clothing), a cap, and safety shoes. Remove gloves, rings, accessories, ties, or other items that may get caught in the machine.
  4. To prevent slipping, tripping, or falling, ensure no hazardous objects are placed around the machine.
  5. Do not place flammable objects near the machine.
  6. Do not operate the machine if the safety devices are not functioning properly.
  7. Ensure the emergency stop button functions correctly.
  8. Do not modify the machine or control circuits without authorization, as this may cause personal injury or machine damage.
  9. Use lubricating, non-corrosive, and non-flammable cutting oils.
  10. Before operating the machine, ensure that tools, collet, etc., are installed correctly.
  11. Before operating the machine, confirm that the coolant level is sufficient and that necessary areas are adequately cooled. Regularly remove swarf from the cutting area and Chip Conveyor.
  12. Do not operate the machine with the protective guard removed!
  13. Do not process flammable, explosive, or dust-generating materials! If such materials need to be processed, additional protective devices must be installed. Consult our technical staff for further information.

Precautions During Operation

  • Do not open the electrical cabinet door while the main circuit breaker is in the "ON" position.
  • During machine operation, do not touch or approach cutting tools, rotating parts, or moving parts of the machine.
  • When operating the machine, visually confirm the operation switches before pressing them.

Precautions for Shutting Down the Machine

Shutdown sequence: Reset/Stop the machine --- Press the emergency stop button on the control panel --- Turn off the system power, confirm that the display is off and the control panel indicators are off --- Cut off the main circuit breaker.

Emergency Handling Measures

  1. In case of fire, earthquake, or lightning:
    • If there is sufficient time:
      Stop the machine immediately, press the NC system power switch, confirm that the LCD display is off and the panel lights are off, then cut off the main circuit breaker.
    • If there is no time: Press the emergency stop button immediately, then cut off the main circuit breaker.
  2. In case of sudden power failure:
    • After a sudden power failure, turn off the main circuit breaker and press the emergency stop button.
  3. Resuming operation after an emergency:
    • After stopping the machine due to an emergency or sudden power failure, restart the machine in the following sequence:Check for tool damage and replace if necessary.
    • Remove any unfinished workpieces from the machine.
    • Restart the machine。

Precautions for Machine Storage and Transportation

  1. The machine is packaged with waterproof materials, and rust preventive oil is applied to key components. The packaging box is designed with anti-vibration and anti-impact measures to ensure safe transportation and storage within a temperature range of -25°C to 55°C. However, the packaging box must not be inverted or tilted beyond 15°, and it should not be subjected to severe impacts or vibrations to avoid damaging internal components.
  2. When the machine is not in operation for an extended period, it is necessary to apply rust preventive oil for protection, disconnect the power supply cable, cover the machine with a plastic film to prevent dust, and seal the power input holes in the electrical enclosure of the machine base to prevent rodent damage.

7.1    Heading 

If “APC” is displayed, it indicates that the battery for the absolute pulse encoder has reached the end of its life. 

Refer to the maintenance manual issued by FANUC for replacing the standard alkaline dry bat-tery.  For the location of the absolute pulse encoder battery, see [Figure A8: Component Over-view]. 

7.2    Heading 

If the screen displays “BAT”, it indicates that the CNC unit battery (lithium battery) has reached the end of its life. 

Refer to the FANUC Reference CD (Maintenance and Troubleshooting Manual) for replacing the lithium battery. 

7.3    Heading 

The CNC unit battery is located on the rear side of the NC unit. (Refer to the Reference CD: Maintenance and Troubleshooting Manual)

Article title: 2 Main Applications and Scope of Use
Article body:
This machine is a high-speed, high-precision CNC lathe with 4-axis interpolated motion control. It integrates mechanical, electrical, and pneumatic systems, capable of automatically performing processes such as external turning, facing, milling, side drilling, grooving, threading, drilling, tapping, boring, and internal threading.

The machine's spindle adopts a built-in motorized spindle structure, eliminating the vibration and instability caused by traditional belt drives.The spindle's maximum speed can reach 8000 rpm. The spindle bearings use high-precision NSK angular contact ball bearings from Japan, ensuring high rotational accuracy, rigidity, and long-term precision retention.

The linear axis motion pairs of the machine use imported high-speed, high-precision ball linear guides and ball screws, offering low friction, high sensitivity, and excellent precision stability. It achieves micron-level feed accuracy while reaching a rapid feed rate of 24 m/min, fully meeting the demands of high-speed machining.

The machine is equipped with 4 side milling live tools, 6 external cutting tools, 4 face drilling tools, and 4 back drilling tools, catering to various processing needs.

By configuring a multi-bar automatic feeder, the machine can achieve continuous loading and unloading of multiple bars, enabling fully automated continuous processing.

This machine is widely applicable to industries such as instruments, light industry, electronics, and medical devices for high-precision, high-speed, and efficient mass production of shaft parts.
