4.1 Unpacking the Machine

After opening the outer packaging, first remove the standard accessories, coolant tank, and other accessories from the bottom plate of the packaging box. Then, remove the four fastening nuts connecting the machine to the bottom plate.

4.2 Machine Lifting and Positioning

  1. Use two steel rods with a diameter of Φ45mm (made of 20# high-quality low-carbon steel, one approximately 2m long and the other 1.5m long, with a safety factor greater than 4). Insert them into the holes of the two M24 lifting rings. As shown in Figure 2, hook the lifting slings onto the steel rods (the safety factor of the slings should be greater than 5, and the slings should not touch any machine components. If necessary, use wooden blocks to securely separate the slings from the machine components or use a dedicated lifting frame).

    Lifting Diagram

  2. Gradually tension the lifting slings while checking the center of gravity of the machine tool to ensure safety.
  3. While the machine tool is still suspended in the air, attach the four provided leveling screws and lock nuts together. Thread the leveling screws into the four foundation bolt holes on the base from the foot installation cavity, ensuring the spherical ends protrude from the bottom surface of the base.

    Foundation Pad Placement Diagram

  4. Place the four provided foundation pads in the correct positions at the machine tool’s installation site as shown in the diagram.

    Lowering and Alignment Digram

  5. Gradually lower the machine tool and adjust its position so that the four leveling screws align with the recesses on the foundation pads.
  6. Gently place the machine tool on the four foundation pads and adjust the leveling screws so that the distance between the bottom surface of the machine tool’s base foot screws and the ground is 35mm.

Warning: The following precautions must be followed during the lifting of the machine tool. Failure to do so may result in the machine tool falling (damage) and causing serious personal injury.

  1. Lifting personnel must hold nationally recognized qualification certificates.
  2. Rolling the machine tool is strictly prohibited.
  3. Anti-slip pads (rubber or cloth) must be added to the parts where the lifting rods and lifting cables come into contact.
  4. Ensure that the lifting cables can bear the weight of the machine tool (approximately 2500kg).
  5. The machine tool should not tilt during lifting, and impacts and vibrations should be avoided as much as possible.

4.3 Forklift Transportation

Machine Tool Forklift Diagram

Warning: The following precautions must be followed during the lifting of the machine tool. Failure to do so may result in the machine tool falling (damage) and causing serious personal injury.

  1. Forklift operators must hold nationally recognized qualification certificates.
  2. The forklift must have a capacity of at least 3 tons.
  3. The machine tool should not tilt severely during forklift transportation, and impacts and vibrations should be avoided as much as possible.
  4. 3 Machine Tool Installation

Foundation Requirements

  1. The foundation for installing the machine tool must have a load-bearing capacity of at least 3000 kg/m², and the thickness of the foundation should not be less than 150 mm.
  2. The foundation must extend outward by more than 300 mm from the perimeter of the machine tool.
  3. The machine tool should be installed on a solid, level ground and should not be affected by vibrations from other machines.
  4. When the machine tool is installed on a floor, the structural strength and vibration frequency of the floor must be thoroughly evaluated and approved by the building design unit or an authoritative department.
  5. The placement of the machine tool should allow sufficient space for operation and maintenance as indicated in the drawings.

Environmental Conditions for Machine Tool Operation

  1. Ambient temperature: 0°C to 45°C during operation; -20°C to 55°C during non-operation; temperature change ≤ 20°C per hour.
  2. Relative humidity: Typically, 10% to 75% (no condensation), short-term (within one month) 5% to 95% (no condensation).
  3. During operation ≤ 0.5G, during non-operation ≤ 1G.
  4. Avoid areas where chips, water, or oil may splash.
  5. Keep away from welding and other electrical machining equipment.
  6. Avoid areas with strong electromagnetic interference.
  7. Avoid direct sunlight exposure.

Coolant System Connection

  1. Push the coolant tank into the lower part of the machine tool from the right side, aligning it with the machine tool's external sheet metal.
  2. Place the chip box into the coolant tank and align it with the machine tool's drainage port.
  3. Cover the chip collection box.
  4. Connect the coolant pump to the machine tool using a hose and secure the connection with a hose clamp.
  5. Plug in the coolant pump power supply.
  6. Plug in the coolant tank liquid level detection switch.
  7. Start the coolant pump briefly to test the rotation direction. If the rotation direction is opposite to the direction marked on the pump, change the power phase sequence to adjust the rotation direction.
  8. Fill the coolant tank with coolant (approximately 200L capacity). (Please select the appropriate coolant based on the material being processed.)
  9. Coolant connection is complete.

Diagram of Machine Tool Coolant System

Machine Tool Lubrication System

The lubrication pump oil tank has a capacity of 2L, with an effective capacity of 1.5L. The recommended lubricant is ISO VG-68. Initially, add 1.5L of lubricant to the lubrication pump. After the machine tool has been running for a period, replenish the lubricant in a timely manner.

Note: The lubricant must be clean; otherwise, it may damage the machine tool's guideways and ball screws.

Machine Tool Air Supply Requirements

  • Air supply connection hose for the machine tool: Φ8mm.
  • Air supply requirements: Pressure 0.5MPa, flow rate 80Nl/min.
  • Ensure the air supply is dry and clean. Regularly drain any residual moisture from the air compressor, and add a secondary filtration device if necessary.

Machine Tool Power Supply Requirements

  • Machine tool power supply voltage: 3-phase 380V±10%, 50Hz±2%
  • Machine tool power: 12kVA (including high voltage: 15kVA).

Note: The machine tool grounding must be secure and reliable. The neutral line of the power supply must not be used as a protective grounding line.

Diagram of Machine Tool External Interfaces

Spindle Cooling Oil

Recommended spindle cooling oil: Great Wall No. 7 spindle oil or Mobil No. 4 spindle oil.

The machine tool spindle uses an internal circulation cooling system. An appropriate amount of spindle oil is added at the factory. During transportation, some spindle oil may be lost. Ensure the spindle cooling oil is sufficient when using the machine tool for the first time.

During normal operation, there is no loss of spindle cooling oil. If abnormal loss of spindle cooling oil is observed, check for leaks in the cooling oil circuit and contact the manufacturer for repair if necessary.

The machine tool is equipped with a professional oil chiller for circulating cooling of the spindle. Refer to the oil chiller's user manual for usage and maintenance instructions.

Regularly clean the external filter screen of the oil chiller.

A clogged filter screen will restrict airflow, reducing the cooling efficiency of the oil chiller and causing it to overload. Prolonged overload operation can damage the oil chiller.

Diagram of Oil Chiller Cleaning

 

Machine Tool Leveling

  • Move the spindle head to the negative limit position of the Z1 axis.
  • Place the leveling plate horizontally above the two guide rails of the Z1 axis.
  • Place a precision level on the leveling plate.
  • Adjust the machine tool's leveling screws until the level reads within 0.04mm/m (both axial and longitudinal adjustments are required).
  • Tighten the lock nuts on the leveling screws.

The machine tool's control panel is located on the swing arm at the front of the machine.

Power On

Press the "Power On" key, the indicator light (green) will turn on, the system will power up, and the "Power Off" key indicator light (red) will turn off.

2

Power Off

Press the "Power Off" key, the indicator light (red) will turn on, the system will power down, and the "Power On" key indicator light (green) will turn off.

3

Cycle Start

Press the "Cycle Start" key, the indicator light will turn on.   The system will execute the current program.

4

Cycle Stop

Press the "Cycle Stop" key, the indicator light will turn on. The system will enter a feed hold state.

5

Emergency Stop

Press this button to put the machine in an emergency stop state, stopping all actions.

The system will alarm "Emergency Stop."

Rotate clockwise to exit the emergency stop state.

6

Spindle Override

Use the corresponding override buttons to adjust the rapid traverse (G00) speed.

7

Rapid Override

Use the corresponding override buttons to adjust the rapid traverse (G00) speed.

8

Feed Override

Use the corresponding override buttons to adjust the feed rate (G01) speed.

9

Manual Pulse Generator(MPG) Simulation

Press this button to enter MPG simulation mode while the LED is turned on.

In this mode, the MPG can control the execution of the CNC program. Press "Cycle Start" to begin the machining program. Rotate the "Handwheel Pulse Generator" to control the program's forward and backward movement. If the handwheel is not rotated, the machine will remain in "Feed Hold" state.

Through this function, it is immediately possible to determine whether the program can or cannot be executed for machining.

This function is a user-friendly way to verify program execution

10

Single Block Execution

Press this button to enter single blockexecution mode.

In this mode, each press of the "Cycle Start" button will execute only one block of the program.

11

Optional Stop

Press this button to enter optional stop mode and the LED is turned on.

In this mode, when the program reaches an "M01" code, the machine will switch from "Auto" mode to "Feed Hold" mode.

Press "Cycle Start" again to continue executing the program after the "M01" code.

12

Block Skip

1.  This button is always on.

2. All program blocks with "/" will be skipped by the system.

When the feeder triggers a material shortage signal, the "Block Skip" function will be disabled,   and the system will execute blocks with "/".

13

Program Loop

3.  Press the "Program Loop" button to enter program loop mode while the LED is turned on.In this mode, the program will automatically loop when it reaches the "M99" code. When the loop mode is off, the program will stop at the "M99" code.

14

Channel Selection

Press, the corresponding LED is turned on.Machine tool executes operations related to the first channel.

 

Press , the corresponding LED is turned on.Machine tool executes operations related to the second channel.

4.  Press , the corresponding LED is turned on.Machine tool executes operations related to both channels simultaneously.

15

Axis Selection

In "Manual" mode, press the corresponding button to control the movement of the linear axes.

16

Spindle Selection

Select the spindle to be manually controlled.

17

Spindle Rotation

In"Manual" and "MDI" modes, manually control the spindle's "Forward," "Stop," and "Reverse" operations. |

18

Coolant

Manually control the "on/off" of the machine tool coolant.

19

Air Blow

Manually control the "on/off" of the machine tool air blow.

20

Work Light

Manually control the "on/off" of the machine tool work light.

21

Conveyor

Manually control the rotation of the machine tool conveyor.

22

Part Ejection

Manually control the part ejection action of the machine tool.

23

Spindle Clamping

Manually control the "clamp/release" of the machine tool spindle collet.

24

Back Spindle Clamping

Manually control the "clamp/release" of the back spindle collet.

25

Material Feeder Connection

Manually activate this button when an automatic feeder is configured.

26

 

MPG mode

Move the toggle switch to the position to enter "MPG" mode. In this state, the machine's linear axes can be controlled

the machine's linear axes can be controlled using the manual pulse generator (handwheel).

 

Automatic Execution Mode

Move the toggle switch to the position to enter"Automatic Execution" mode.

In this mode, press the "Cycle Start" button,

and the machine will automatically execute the user-selected program.

 

Manual Continuous Mode

Move the toggle switch to the position to enter"Manual Continuous" mode.

In this state, the machine's linear axes can be controlledusing the directional keys on the operation panel.

 

MDI

Move the toggle switch to the position to enterthe MDI mode.

In this mode, users can perform "Manual Data Input "operations on the machine.

 

Reference Point Return

Move the toggle switch to the position to enter"Reference Point Return" mode.

In this state, the machine can perform homing operations for each axis.

6.6 Application of Main M-codes

Spindle Control (M03, M04, M05, M50, M51)

Controls spindle rotation (CW/CCW), stop,and operation modes (speed/position).

  • Command Format

M03 S□□□□□ Spindle Start (Clockwise)

M 04 S□□□□□             Spindle counterclockwise rotation

M 05    Spindle Stop

M 50    Spindle C-axis control

M 51    Spindle RPM Control

Programming Example:

┆

M 10    // Clamp spindle Collet

┆

M 03S 3000    // Spindle CW rotation at 3000r/min

┆

M 05    // Spindle stop

┆

M 04S 3000    // Spindle CCW rotation at 3000r/min

┆

M 05    // Spindle stop

M 50    // Switch to c-axis control mode

G 00 C0          // Rapid positioning to C=0

G 01 C180 .F1000      // Rotate to C180 at F1000 speed M 51         // Switch to RPM mode

┆

Sub-Spindle Control (M23, M24, M25, M38, M39)

Controls sub-spindle rotation (CW/CCW), stop, and operation modes.

  • Command Format

M23 S2 =□□□□□         Sub-spindle CW rotation

M 24 S2=□□□□□         Sub-spindle CCW rotation

M 25    Sub Spindle Stop

M 38    Sub spindle C-axis control

M 39    Sub-Spindle RPM Control

┆

M 23 S2= 3000           //Sub-spindle CW rotation at 3000r/min

┆

┆

┆

M 38    // Switch to c-axis control mode

G 01 C180 .F1000      //Rotate to C180 at F1000 speed   M 39        // Switch to RPM mode

┆

┆

Spindle Collet Control (M10, M11)

Pneumatic clamp/unclamp control for spindle collet.

The machine tool control panel is equipped with manual spindle collet clamping/unclamping buttons. When the machine operates in automatic mode, these manual buttons are disabled, and spindle collet clamping/unclamping actions are controlled exclusively by the corresponding M-codes.

  • Command Format

M10     Clamp spindle collet

M11     Unclamp spindle collet

When executing the M11 command during spindle acceleration/deceleration, the spindle unclamping action will commence only after the spindle rotation has fully stopped or dropped below the designated speed threshold.

Sub-Spindle Collet Control (M20, M21)

Clamp or unclamp the sub-spindle collet.

Pneumatic control for sub-spindle collet.

The machine tool control panel is equipped with manual sub-spindle collet clamping/unclamping buttons. When the machine operates in automatic mode, these manual buttons are disabled, and sub-spindle collet clamping/unclamping actions are controlled exclusively by the corresponding M-codes.

  • Command Format

M20     Sub Spindle Collet Clamp

M21     Unclamp sub-spindle collet

When executing the M21 command during sub-spindle acceleration/deceleration, the spindle unclamping action will commence only after the spindle rotation has fully stopped or dropped below the designated speed threshold.

Coolant Control (M08, M09)

The coolant system of this machine tool is driven by a three-phase motor. When the machine is in manual mode, the coolant is turned on or off by the button switch on the operation panel. When the machine is in automatic operation mode, the coolant is controlled by M codes.

  • Command Format

M08: Coolant On

M09: Coolant Off

HP Coolant Control (Optional) (M104, M105, M106, M107, M108, M109, M110, M111)

This machine tool can be equipped with a high-pressure coolant system, which effectively removes chips wrapped around the cutting tool. The high-pressure coolant system draws cutting oil from the coolant tank. When using the high-pressure coolant system, the fluid level in the tank will be lower than usual. If the coolant tank is filled to capacity, stopping the coolant may cause the tank to overflow. Therefore, when using the high-pressure coolant system, ensure the coolant level is appropriate. The high-pressure coolant system has four coolant circuits. If two or more circuits are opened simultaneously, the fluid pressure from the nozzles may decrease.

The high-pressure coolant circuits are controlled by corresponding M codes.

  • Command Format

M 104 HP Circuit 1 On

M 105 HP Circuit 1 Off

M 106 HP Circuit 2 On

M 107 HP Circuit 2 Off

M 108 HP Circuit 3 On

M 109 HP Circuit 3 Off

M 110 HP Circuit 4 On

M 111 HP Circuit 4 Off

Ejector Control (M17, M18)

The extension and retraction of the ejector rod on this machine tool are controlled by a pneumatic valve.

When the machine is in manual mode, the ejector rod can be extended or retracted using the ejector button on the operation panel. When the machine is in automatic machining mode, the corresponding M codes control the extension and retraction of the ejector rod.

  • Command Format

M 17    Ejector Rod Extend

M 18    Ejector Rod Retract

Note:

  1. If the ejector rod is extended (M17) while the sub-spindle collet is clamped or the sub-spindle is running, an alarm will be triggered.
  2. Do not clamp the sub-spindle (M20) while the ejector rod is extended, as this will trigger an alarm.
  3. The ejector cylinder is equipped with an extension detection switch. If the switch does not detect the relevant signal when the ejector rod is extended (M17), an alarm will be triggered.

Parts Conveyor (M26, M27)

This machine tool is equipped with a parts conveyor that transfers parts ejected from the sub-spindle to an external collection box.

Maximum part diameter: ≤ Φ26 mm Maximum part length: ≤ 80 mm Conveyor belt width:          78 mm

  • Command Format

M 26    Parts Conveyor On

M 27    Parts Conveyor Off

Note:

  1. Depending on the part geometry, some parts may not be ejected.
  2. When executing the M26 command, the conveyor belt operates for approximately 15 seconds and then stops automatically. There is no need to use the M27 command to stop the conveyor belt.
  3. The operating time of the conveyor belt can be adjusted by modifying parameter 3432.

6.6.9 Sub-Spindle Air Blow Control (M28, M29)

The sub-spindle air blow function clears chips remaining on the sub-spindle collet.

  • Command Format

M 17    Sub Spindle Air Blow On

M 18    Sub Spindle Air Blow Off

Note:              

  1. The air blow pipeline is equipped with a flow control valve, which may affect the air volume and pressure, impacting the removal of chips from the sub-spindle collet.
  2. Do not remove the flow control valve directly, as this may cause a rapid drop in system pressure when the air blow is activated, affecting the normal operation of the machine. If increased air volume is required, connect an external air source for the sub-spindle air blow.
  3. Simultaneously executing the air blow and ejector rod extension (M17) and retraction (M18) can improve the removal of chips from the sub-spindle collet.
  4. Rotating the sub-spindle at low speed while executing the air blow can also improve chip removal.

Lateral Live Tool Control (M33, M34, M35)

Controls the rotation (forward or reverse) and stopping of the lateral live tool.

This machine tool is equipped with four lateral live tools, which can perform lateral milling, lateral drilling, and lateral tapping. Tools T11 and T12 can be fitted with ER11 collets, while tools T13 and T14 can be fitted with ER16 collets

  • Command Format

M 33 S3=□□□□□ Live Tool Forward Rotation

M 34 S3=□□□□□ Live Tool Reverse Rotation

M 35    Lateral Live Tool Stop

Note:

  1. The gear ratio between the tool spindle and motor for T11 and T12 is 30:26. The relationship between the tool spindle speed and motor speed is as follows:
    Tool Spindle Speed=Motor Speed 15/13
  2. The gear ratio between the tool spindle and motor for T13 and T14 is 1:1, meaning the tool spindle speed is the same as the motor speed.
  3. When using lateral live tools for machining, feed per minute (G98) is typically used to specify the tool feed rate.
  4. If feed per revolution (G99) is required to specify the feed rate or if the lateral live tool tapping function is needed, execute the M83 code to designate the live tool spindle as the main spindle.

Back Live Tool Control (Optional) (M93, M94, M95)

Specifies the rotation (forward or reverse) and stopping of the Back live tools.

This machine can be optionally equipped with 3 Back live tools. By configuring Back live tools, operations such as Back eccentric hole drilling, Back slot milling, and face milling can be performed. The 3 Back live tools are installed in tool positions T32, T33, and T34, replacing the standard Back drilling tools. The Back live tools are equipped with ER16 collets.

  • Command Format

M 93 S5=□□□□□ Back live tool forward rotation

M 94 S5=□□□□□   Back live tool reverse rotation

M 95    Back Live Tool Stop

Note:

  1. The transmission ratio between the Back live tool spindle and the motor is 5:3. The relationship between the tool spindle speed and the motor speed is as follows:
    Tool Spindle Speed=Motor Speed 5/3
  2. When using lateral live tools for machining, the feed rate is typically specified using G98 (feed per minute).
  3. If G99 (feed per revolution) is required to specify the feed rate for live tools or if the lateral live tool tapping function is needed, the M85 code must be executed to designate the live tool spindle as the working spindle.

Designate Working Spindle (M81, M82, M83, M85)

By default, the machine system designates the first channel spindle as the working spindle, and the second channel sub-spindle as the working spindle. When performing operations such as lateral tapping, Back tapping, or interpolation with other spindles, the corresponding M code must be executed to designate the appropriate spindle as the working spindle.

After completing machining with another designated working spindle, the corresponding M code must be executed to switch the working spindle back to the main spindle or sub-spindle.

  • Command Format

M 81    Designate main spindle as working spindle

M 82    Designate sub-spindle as working spindle

M 83    Designate lateral live tool spindle as working spindle

M 85    Designate Back live tool spindle as working spindle

6.6.13 Automatic Cut-Off (G266 A* X* S* T* P* F* B* W*)

During the machining process, if an abnormal condition occurs (e.g., tool life expiration, tool breakage, machine fault), the machine must be stopped. After resolving the issue, a cut-off operation is required before restarting the machining process. The G266 command can be used to perform an automatic cut-off operation.

    Dual-Spindle Synchronization Function (G200, G113)

    Synchronizes the speed and phase of the main spindle and sub-spindle. In this mode, the machine can perform the following operations:

        1. Transferring a workpiece from the main spindle to the sub-spindle.
        2. Simultaneously holding and machining a single workpiece with both the main spindle and sub-spindle.
        3. Maintaining phase synchronization between the main spindle and sub-spindle for machining irregularly shaped parts.
        • Command Format

    G 200 S□□□□             Synchronizes the main spindle and sub-spindle to rotate at speed S□□□□

    G113               Cancels the dual-spindle synchronization function

    Dual Z-Axis Coupling Function (M1000, M1001)

    Couples the Z2 axis with the Z1 axis, enabling the Z2 axis to follow the Z1 axis in synchronized motion. In this mode, the machine can perform the following operations:

        1. Simultaneously holding and performing external turning on a single workpiece with the main spindle and sub-spindle.
        2. Simultaneously holding and performing side milling on a single workpiece with the main spindle and sub-spindle.
        3. Simultaneously holding and performing external thread turning on a single workpiece with the main spindle and sub-spindle.
        • Command Format

    M 1000            Enables Z1 and Z2 axis coupling

    M1001                         Disables Z1 and Z2 axis coupling.

    Dual C-Axis Coupling Function (M1002, M1003)

    Same as above

    System Wait Function (G04.1)

    • Command Format G 04. 1 P□□

    P□□ Wait signal code.

    The numbers following P must be assigned in ascending order.

    1. Enable this function during drilling operations on the main/sub-spindle. If a drill breaks, the Z1/Z2 motor load spikes instantly, triggering an alarm to prompt tool replacement and avoid further damage.
    2. Enable before sub-spindle synchronization. If chip entanglement on the main spindle or residual parts in the sub-spindle collet hinder synchronization, the Z1/Z2 load spikes, alerting operators to inspect and resolve issues.
    3. Enable for irregular materials during synchronization. A phase misalignment between spindles increases Z1/Z2 load, prompting correction of synchronization phase deviation.
    4. Enable after synchronized cut-off. If the tool is broken and the workpiece remains uncut, sub-spindle retraction increases Z1/Z2 load, alerting operators to replace the tool.

    Machine Tool Control (T Command)

    Tool changes are executed via servo axes controlled by the system. The T-code specifies the tool number in the program.

    • Cmmand Format

                T □□△△

                □□：Tool Number

    • Turret Tool（T01～T09）
    • Lateral Live Tool（T11～T19）
    • Face Tool（T21～T29）
    • Back Tool（T31～T39）

    △△：Tool Nose & Wear Offset ID

    The4 -digit T code positions the tool:. The first 2 digits indicate the tool number, and the last 2 digits denote compensation. T0000 cancels compensation. The X、Y、Z coordinates for each tool (tool number) are stored in the system's tool length registry.

    Note:                  

    1. Tool selection defaults to rapid feed motion.
    2. T codes must be in standalone program blocks.
    3. Do not combine T-codes (T□□△△) with M/S-codes in the same block.
    4. Unauthorized personnel must not modify tool length values. Always back up data before changes.

    6.7 Machine Setup and Adjustment Instructions

    Machine Linear Axes

    The machine is equipped with 5 servo-driven linear axes:Y1 and X2 axes: Perform tool-changing motions.X axis: Controls radial positioning and radial cutting feed.Z1 and Z2 axes: Control axial positioning and axial cutting feed. All servo axes utilize absolute encoders for position feedback.

    Spindle Rotation Direction

    When facing the spindle from the right side of the machine:Counterclockwise (CCW) rotation is defined as spindle forward (M03).Clockwise (CW) rotation is defined as spindle reverse (M04).

    Tool Specifications

    1. OD Turning Tool Holders: 6× □12×100 mm square shank tools(G2 Model).5× □12×100 mm + 1× □16×100 mm square shank tools（SL265 Model)
    2. Face Drilling Tool Holder Shank: Φ22 mm.
    3. Face Drilling Axis: Equipped with ER16 collets.
    4. Side Milling Live Tool Axis: 2× ER11 collets + 2× ER16 collets.
    5. Back Drilling Axis: ER16 collets.

    5.8 Shutdown Procedures

    1. Normal Shutdown During Operation
      During automatic program execution:Press the Cycle Stop button on the operator panel to activate M01 (Optional Stop).When the program pauses at M01, press the RESET key to halt the machine.
    2. End-of-Day Shutdown
      Follow the Normal Shutdown steps above.Press the red System Power Off button on the operator panel. Confirm the display and indicators are off.Turn the main power breaker to OFF.
    3. Emergency Shutdown
      Press the E-stop button (on the operator panel or front control box).Press the red System Power Off button.Turn the main power breaker to OFF.

    WARNING: Avoid using the E-stop during normal operation. Emergency stops may damage workpieces, tools, or machine components.

    6.9 Pre-Machining Preparations

    1. Turn on Air Supply: Ensure the pneumatic system pressure is 0.5 MPa ±10%.
    2. Lubrication: Verify the lubricant level in the lubrication unit exceeds the minimum mark.
    3. Coolant (if required): Ensure the coolant tank level exceeds the minimum mark.
    4. Confirm the factory voltage matches machine requirements. Turn the main power breaker to ON.
    5. Close the machine tool power circuit breaker to the "ON" position.
    6. Press the "System On" button on the upper part of the operation panel to start the CNC system.
    7. Reset (rotate to the right) the emergency stop button on the main operation panel and front operation box.
    8. If coolant is needed, confirm that the cooling system can work normally: press the "Coolant" button to turn on the coolant, then press the "Coolant" button again to turn off the coolant.

    6.10 Installation of Main Spindle Collet and Adjustment of Clamping Force

    Spindle Collet Installation

    Clamping Mechanism Diagram

    Adjustment Steps:

    1. Loosen the spindle locknut (Back) counterclockwise to release the collet.
    2. Install the taper sleeve
    3. Install the spring
    4. Install the spindle collet
    5. Install and tighten the spindle nut.
    6. Insert the bar stock through the collet, extending beyond the spindle face.
    7. Clamp the spindle collet.
    8. Rotate the locknut at the Back end of the spindle clockwise until the collet just clamps the bar.
    9. Release the spindle collet.
    10. Continue to rotate the locknut at the Back end of the spindle clockwise about 1/4 turn
    11. Clamp the spindle collet and check if the clamping force is appropriate. Fine-tune the locking nut at the Back end of the spindle until the spindle has the appropriate clamping force.
    12. Tighten the anti-loosening screw on the side of the locking nut at the Back end of the spindle.
    13. Confirm the position of the jaws on the sliding sleeve during clamping, and adjust the relative position of the jaws on the sliding sleeve through the connecting rod on the clamping cylinder.
    14. Tighten the connecting rod and the nut on the cylinder.

    6.11 Guide Sleeve Collet Installation and Clamping Force Adjustment

    1. Use a special hex wrench to confirm that the locking screw on the left edge of the collet locking nut has been loosened.
    2. Clean the installation hole of the guide sleeve collet and the guide sleeve collet, and add lubricating oil.
    3. Install the guide sleeve collet into the guide sleeve, aligning the slot on the guide sleeve collet with the pin phase inside the guide sleeve.
    4. Load the bar stock into the Guide Sleeveing, extending it beyond the face of the guide sleeve collet Use a special wrench to rotate the guide sleeve collet locking nut. Gradually tighten the guide sleeve collet.
    5. When tightening the guide sleeve collet, do not clamp the bar too tightly; the bar should be able to be pulled flexibly by hand (otherwise, the collet will be burned). There must be no significant gap between the bar stock and the Guide Sleeveing collet; otherwise, the part’s machining quality will deteriorate.
    6. After adjusting the clamping force of the guide sleeve collet appropriately, use a special hex wrench to tighten the locking screw. Check the clamping force of the guide sleeve collet again. If the clamping force is not appropriate, repeat the above steps.

    6.12 Application of Bushingless Mode

    This machine tool is capable of operating in bushingless mode.

    For parts with a length-to-diameter ratio of less than 3, the bushingless mode can be selected to effectively reduce the length of the remaining material.

    Maximum part length for bushingless mode: 50mm.

    Before using the machine, the bushing mode must be correctly configured. Different bushing modes will invoke different Z1 -axis soft limits.

    On the initial page of the NC system, navigate to the right and find the "SYIL" function option in the last item. Within the "SYIL" function option, locate the "Bushing" setting option. Use the up and down arrow keys to select the appropriate bushing mode.

    Rotary Guide Sleeve Transmission Diagram

    Bushingless Machining Diagram

    Steps to Replace Rotary Bushing with Bushingless Machining:

    1. Remove the spindle nut at the front end of the spindle.
    2. Remove the spline belt guard plate.
    3. Remove the protective cover at the front end of the spindle.
    4. Disengage the spindle drive belt.
    5. Disengage the spline nut fixing bracket (two M8 screws connected to the spindle housing).
    6. Fix the spline nut fixing bracket to the column.
    7. Remove the guard plate on the side of the rotary guide sleeve.
    8. Disengage the rotary guide sleeve drive belt.
    9. Remove the rotary guide sleeve assembly.
    10. Install the protective sleeve for the bushingless device.
    11. Install the front protective cover of the spindle.
    12. Install the guard plate on the side of the bushing seat.
    13. Reinstall the spindle nut.
    14. Set the machine to bushingless mode in the CNC system. The switch is now complete.

    Installation of External Cutting Tools

    1. Use the mode selection toggle switch on the control panel to select "MDI" mode.
      Use the NC function key to select the "Machining Info" menu and enter the MDI machining info page.
      Use the NC function key to select the "MDI Input" menu to bring up the MDI input page.
    2. Press       ，button on the control panel to illuminate it.
    3. Enter the following program code (example for installing T02 Φ16mm): T0202 //Call T02 tool
      G00 Y0
      G00 X16.0
    4. Extend the material approximately 15mm beyond the bushing end face.
    5. Loosen the tool clamp screw.
    6. Insert the tool into the tool slot, ensuring it is flush with the bottom of the slot.
    7. Gently tighten the tool clamp screw to prevent the tool from falling out.
    8. Use the mode selection switch on the control panel to switch the machine to MPG mode.
    9. Move the carriage in the "X+" direction to separate the tool from the material.
    10. Tighten the tool clamp screw to secure the tool.

    Installation of Front Drilling Tools

    1. Assemble the drilling tool with the drilling spindle.
    2. Insert the drilling tool and spindle assembly into the drilling tool holder.
    3. Use a dedicated drilling tool alignment fixture to position the drilling tool assembly.
    4. Tighten the locking screw on the side of the drilling tool holder to secure the drilling tool assembly.

    Installation of Side Milling Live Tools

    1. Use the mode selection toggle switch on the control panel to select "MDI" mode.
    2. Use the NC function key to select the "Machining Info" menu and enter the MDI machining info page.
    3. Use the NC function key to select the "MDI Input" menu to bring up the MDI input page.
    4. Press     ，button on the control panel to illuminate it.
    5. Enter the following program：（example for installing T11 tool，Φ16mm） T1111
      G00 Y0 G00 X16.0
    6. Install the corresponding side live tool in the T11 tool position (do not tighten yet).
    7. Extend the bar stock beyond the end face of the Guide Sleeveing collet
    8. Pull the side live tool out of the power spindle until it contacts the outer diameter of the bar stock.
    9. Use a dedicated wrench to gently tighten the ER collet nut to prevent the tool from falling out.
    10. Use the mode selection switch on the control panel to switch the machine to MPG mode.
    11. Move the carriage in the "X+" direction to separate the tool from the material.
    12. Tighten the corresponding ER collet nut to secure the tool.

    Warning: When installing live tools, observe the following precautions to avoid personal injury or machine damage:

    • Ensure the power tool spindle is stopped before installing the tool.
    • Remove all tools (e.g., dedicated wrenches) after installation.

    Precautions for Using Power Tools:

    1. T11 and T12 live tools are compatible with ER11 collets.
    2. T13 and T14 live tools are compatible with ER16 collets.
    3. The center of T11 and T12 power tools is 10mm from the bushing face.
    4. The center of T13 and T14 power tools is 15mm from the bushing face.

    6.14 Use of the Feeder

    This machine can be equipped with an automatic multi-bar feeder according to processing needs. During long-term mass production, manual material replacement is no longer required, as the bar feeder automatically completes the task.

    Bar Feeder-Related M Codes

    M60:    Activate the bar feeder material change cycle

    M61:    Spindle jog – ON

    M62:    Spindle jog – OFF

    M63:    Bar feeder thrust-ON

    M64:    Bar feeder thrust-OFF

    M65:    Bar feeder retract allowed – ON

    M66:    Bar feeder retract allowed – OFF

    Precautions for Using the Bar Feeder

    1. Adjust the bar feeder thrust appropriately based on the size, material, and weight of the bar stock.
    2. Refer to the relevant bar feeder manual for setting bar feeder parameters.
    3. Ensure the collet at the front of the push rod rotates smoothly.
    4. Select appropriate feed channel assembly and ejector rod accessories corresponding to the diameter of the machined bar stock
    5. If necessary, install a vibration-damping bushing on the spindle based on the size of the bar stock.

    6.15 Adjustment of the Ejector Device

    The ejector device is used to discharge workpieces from the back spindle To handle situations where the workpiece cannot be discharged smoothly after the eject command, a safety device with stop alarm is equipped. When replacing the ejector head, select one that matches the diameter of the workpiece. If the standard ejector head does not meet the processing requirements, the user must fabricate a custom ejector head.

    Diagram of Ejector Device

    1. Air Blow Connector 2. Adjustment Screw 3. Support Sleeve 4. Locking Screw 5. Ejector Tube 6. Support Tube 7. Ejector Head 8. Connection Plate

    Figure 29: Diagram of Ejector Device

    Installation and Adjustment of Ejector Device

    1. Insert the (6) support tube into the (3) support sleeve.
    2. Tighten the (4) locking screw.
    3. Mount the assembled (3) support sleeve and (6) support tube onto the spindle bracket, and tighten the fixing screws of the support sleeve.
    4. Adjust the (2) adjustment screw to align the support tube concentrically with the spindle bore.
    5. Assemble the (5) ejector tube with the (8) connection plate.
    6. Attach the air blow connector to the (5) ejector tube.
    7. Install the (7) ejector head onto the (5) ejector tube.
    8. Insert the (5) ejector tube assembly into the (6) support tube, and slide the (5) ejector tube back and forth to ensure (5) ejector tube smooth movement.
    9. Mount the (8) connection plate onto the ejector cylinder.
    10. Operate the ejector cylinder to move the (5) ejector tube. When the (5) ejector tube extends, adjust its position relative to the (8) connection plate to ensure the ejector head protrudes 1–2mm from the back spindle chuck face. Tighten the locking screws on the (8) connection plate to secure the ejector tube.
    11. Repeatedly extend and retract the ejector cylinder to ensure smooth operation without jamming. Secure the (8) connection plate to the ejector cylinder.

    Note: If the (5) ejector tube jams during operation, an ejector malfunction alarm will be triggered. Adjustthe concentricity of the (6) support tube relative to the spindle bore to ensure smooth ejector tube movement.

    Ejector Head

    Figure 30 Diagram of Ejector Head

    The user should adjust the φ16 dimension at the end of the ejector head in Figure 30 according to the size of the workpiece to meet processing requirements.

    A device used for retrieving workpieces with a length exceeding 100mm. The workpiece is inserted into the long workpiece storage tube inside the back spindle and then retrieved from the long workpiece collection box on the right side of the machine. It is necessary to prepare a long workpiece storage tube and guide sleeve corresponding to the workpiece.

    4. Adjust the (2) adjustment screw to ensure the (1) storage tube is concentric with the inner bore of the spindle.

    6.18 Part Machining Procedure

    The procedure for formal part machining is shown in Figure 8.

    Figure 33 Part Machining Flowchart

    Special attention should be paid to the following items during the part machining procedure:

    1. If there is an error in the part machining program, it must be corrected before continuing to run the program.
    2. After programming, the program must be run without tools installed to perform a dry run. Only after the dry run is error-free can subsequent work continue.
    3. Install the tools and the part blank. Position each tool to the pre-feed position according to the programming data and confirm if there is any possibility of interference or tool collision.
    4. Press the "Single Block" button on the operation panel, lower the feed rate override knob value, and run the program in single block mode. Only after the previous block runs without errors can the next block be executed. During single block operation, closely monitor for any command errors or tool offset setting errors that may cause discrepancies. If any potential error actions are detected, immediately press the emergency stop button, exit the operation, and resolve all errors before resuming automatic operation.

To predict machine failures or detect them early and minimize production losses, regular inspections of the machine should be conducted regardless of whether there are any faults. This chapter summarizes and explains the necessary inspection items from daily checks to those required every six months.

7.1 Routine Maintenance

Daily inspections are simple checks that should be performed before and during the operation of the machine each day. Please follow the inspection points listed below. Additionally, note that the situations requiring attention are not limited to those listed in this chapter. If the machine exhibits abnormal conditions, or if there are noticeable abnormal sounds, temperatures, damages, or odors during operation, inspections must be conducted immediately. Therefore, it is essential to understand the normal operating state of the machine, which is the most effective daily inspection.

7.2 Common Faults and Solutions

This chapter introduces the handling methods for common faults. The methods provided are for common issues, but in practice, faults beyond these may occur. If the cause cannot be identified or resolved, please contact SYIL Customer Service Center for repair.

Main/Sub-Spindle Collet Failure to Open/Close and Timeout Alarm

If the spindle chuck fails to open, it may result in short workpieces or failure to eject the bar stock.

  • Handling Steps:
    1. Check if the air supply is normal.
    2. Check if the clamping solenoid valve is functioning properly.
    3. Check if the clamping cylinder is functioning properly.
    4. Check if the clamping sensor switch is functioning properly and if it is loose or damaged.
    5. Check if the clamping cylinder connecting rod is loose.
    6. Check if the chuck jaws' travel is normal.
    7. Disassemble the spindle nut, clean the collet and collet cone, and clean the spindle bore.
    8. Check if the collet cone is scratched or if it is stuck in the spindle bore.
    9. Replace the collet with a new one.

If the issue persists after these checks, contact SYIL Customer Service Center for repair.

Low Coolant Flow

The machine uses cutting oil for cooling. Insufficient cooling may pose a fire hazard. Address low coolant flow promptly.

  • Handling Steps:
    1. Check if the coolant pipes are detached.
    2. Check if the coolant pump is functioning properly.
    3. Check if the coolant tank has sufficient oil.
    4. Clean the chip collection cavity in the machine base.

Chip Conveyor and Coolant Tank Diagram

Spindle/Sub-spindle Overheat Alarm

The spindle and sub-spindle of this machine are equipped with built-in motor spindles with temperature sensors. An alarm will trigger if the spindle temperature is too high.

  • Handling Steps:
    1. Check the I/O input module's I16 (spindle overheat detection) and I23 (sub-spindle overheat detection) inputs. A faulty module may cause false alarms. Contact SYIL Customer Service Center if the module is damaged.
    2. Check if the spindle temperature is abnormally high and if there is a burning smell. If so, the spindle stator may be damaged. Contact SYIL Customer Service Center.
    3. Check if the spindle speed fluctuates abnormally. If so, the spindle stator or encoder may be damaged. Contact SYIL Customer Service Center.
    4. Check if the spindle load fluctuates abnormally during idle operation. If so, the spindle stator may be damaged. Contact SYIL Customer Service Center.
    5. Check if the spindle's three-phase resistance is balanced. If not, the spindle stator may be damaged. Contact SYIL Customer Service Center.
    6. Check the resistance between the spindle's three-phase coils and the spindle housing. If any phase shows finite resistance, the spindle coils may be shorted to the housing. Contact SYIL Customer Service Center.
    7. Check if the spindle power cables are damaged or there is a phase loss.
    8. Check if the spindle drive unit is damaged.

Ejector Malfunction Alarm

The sub-spindle is equipped with an ejector for parts shorter than 80mm.

If the part is not ejected, it may interfere with subsequent parts or machine components, causing a crash.

For the configuration and calibration of the ejector device, refer to Section 6.16: Installation and Adjustment of the Ejector System.

During ejector operation, if the machine control system fails to detect the signal from the ejector cylinder proximity sensor, a corresponding alarm will be triggered.

  • Handling Steps:
    1. Check if the air pressure is normal.
    2. Check if the ejector solenoid valve is functioning properly.
    3. Check if the air hoses are damaged.
    4. Check if the ejector cylinder's throttle valve is adjusted properly.
    5. Check if the ejector cylinder operates smoothly without sticking.
    6. Check if the ejector sensor switch is loose or damaged.
    7. Check if the ejector rod is bent or stuck.
    8. Check if there are any residual workpieces or chips in the sub-spindle and clean them.

Torque Limit Exceeded Alarm

During machining, if a drill bit breaks, a tool wears severely, or a cutting tool is damaged, the torque on the axis may spike, risking a crash. Adjusting the torque limits can help prevent such incidents.

Adjust axis torque output limits based on specific machining requirements. Adjust the torque limit parameters as needed.

Lost Position Command Alarm

  • Alarm Description

The controller stops outputting commands to an axis for 1 second and checks if the feedback matches the command within the allowed error range. If not, this alarm is triggered.

  • Possible Causes
    1. Mechanical obstruction.
    2. Servo drive unexpectedly enabled or disabled.
    3. Data loss between the CPU board and Axis Control Card due to CPU/Card failure or poor contact.
    4. Poor contact or broken wires in the command transmission line between the controller and servo drive.
    5. Local electromagnetic interference.
  • Troubleshooting Steps:
    1. Check the lubrication system and ensure the guideways are properly lubricated.
    2. Manually rotate the lead screw to check for jamming.
    3. Check the motor power and encoder cables for proper connection.
    4. Check for damage in the motor power and encoder cables.
    5. Check if the drive is powered properly and if there is any phase loss.
    6. After the alarm, without shutting down, check if diagnostic data 8, 9, and 10 converge to 0. If not, re-home the axis and observe diagnostic data 24, 25, 26, 40, 41, and 42. If any of 24, 25, or 26 are non-zero, there is a feedback loop issue.

      If any of 40, 41, or 42 are non-zero, there is a command loss issue.
      If both sets have non-zero values, signal interference is likely. Specifically, during machining, the values of variables 8, 9, and 10 gradually increase. The possible fault point may be poor contact between the CPU board and the axis control card. Try replacing the CPU board and the axis control card.

    7. The motor or drive may be damaged. Try replacing them.

Following Error Alarm, Severe Following Error Alarm

  • Alarm Description
    1. Due to servo characteristics, the servo motor's positioning may lag behind the controller's command. If this lag exceeds the allowed range, a following error alarm is triggered.
  • Possible Causes
    1. Mechanical obstruction.
    2. Poor cable contact
    3. Controller acceleration/deceleration time is set too short.
    4. Drive or motor failure.
  • Troubleshooting Steps:
    1. Troubleshooting Steps:Check the lubrication system and ensure all lubrication points are properly lubricated.
    2. Check the machine's wiring to ensure all connections are secure and undamaged.
    3. Adjust the acceleration/deceleration time for the affected axis.
    4. Run the machine idle to confirm smooth operation without interference.
    5. If the alarm persists, contact SYIL Customer Service Center.

Note:

  1. Diagnostic variable 32: X-axis allowable following error.
  2. Diagnostic variable 33: Y-axis allowable following error.
  3. Diagnostic variable 34: Z-axis allowable following error.

Low Absolute Position Encoder Battery

The servo motors of this machine tool are equipped with absolute position encoders. After the machine is powered off and restarted, there is no need to perform a reference point return operation, and machining can be directly carried out. When the machine is powered off, the servo motor encoders record the position information of each linear axis. The power for the servo motor encoders is supplied by a battery located in the electrical cabinet at the rear of the machine. When the battery voltage is low, the system will issue a "Low Absolute Position Encoder Battery" alarm. Please replace the battery promptly.

  • Precautions for Battery Replacement
    1. Battery Specification: LR20 type (i.e., D-cell battery).
    2. The battery must be replaced while the machine is powered on; otherwise, the absolute position data will be lost.

Battery Compartment Location Diagram

Absolute Position Zero Point Lost

The servo motors of this machine tool are equipped with absolute position encoders. After the machine is powered off and restarted, there is no need to perform a reference point return operation, and machining can be directly carried out. When the machine is powered off, the servo motor encoders record the position information of each linear axis.   If the system fails to read the position data from the servo motor encoders when the machine is powered on, this alarm will be triggered.

  • Possible Causes
    1. The machine has been idle for an extended period, and the absolute position encoder battery has been depleted.
    2. The absolute position encoder battery was not replaced in time when it was low.
    3. The motor or drive unit is damaged.
    4. A new motor has been installed.
  • Troubleshooting Steps:
    1. If the machine is not used for an extended period, it should be powered on at least once a week.
    2. Replace the absolute position encoder battery promptly when it is low.
    3. Reset the absolute position zero point.

Machine Zero Point Reference: Refer to Section 5.6 the machine travel diagram.

Z1 Axis Zero Point: 5mm from the positive hard limit block of the Z1 axis.

X1 Axis Zero Point: The center of the T25 drilling tool position coincides with the spindle center. Y1 Axis Zero Point: The right face of the T01 tool coincides with the spindle center.

X2 Axis Zero Point: The sub-spindle center coincides with the spindle center Z2 Axis Zero Point: 5mm from the negative hard limit block of the Z2 axis.

  • Steps to Set the Zero Point:
    1. Use the rotary switch on the control panel to switch the machine to MPG control mode.
    2. Use the MPG to move each linear axis to the origin position indicated in the machine travel diagram in Section 5.6.
    3. Use the rotary switch on the control panel to switch the machine to reference point mode.
    4. Use the NC function keys to navigate: "Parameter Setting" → "Serial Parameters" → "General Serial Axis" → "Absolute Zero Point" to enter the mechanical zero point setting interface.
    5. Move the cursor to select the corresponding axis number and complete the zero point setting.

7.3 Maintenance Precautions

  1. Qualified maintenance personnel should familiarize themselves with the electrical control principles and operating procedures of the machine before performing any repairs.
  2. Regularly clean the dust inside the electrical enclosure during machine operation.
  3. Ensure the power is turned off before cleaning.If the machine is idle for an extended period, power it on at least once a week and let it run at low speed forno less than 2 hours.
  4. When a fault occurs, first check whether the power supply and control voltages are normal, whether the circuit breakers and switches are in the correct state, and whether all connectors and plugs are securely connected and not loose.
  5. Do not plug or unplug any connectors while the machine is powered on.
  6. Do not solder electronic circuits while the soldering iron is powered on.
  7. The positions of switches and limit blocks on the machine are pre-adjusted before leaving the factory. Users should not adjust them casually to avoid affecting normal operation.

Note: If the user cannot resolve the fault, please contact our company promptly for proper handling.

The machine tool is a CNC lathe controlled by a 64-bit microprocessor. The CNC axis is a 4 linkage control. It is a machine, electric, and gas-body product. It can automatically complete the turning of outer circles, end faces, milling flats, side drilling, processing of grooving, thread-ing, drilling, tapping, boring, internal threading, etc.

The machine tool spindle adopts a built-in spindle motor structure (electric spindle), which elim-inates the vibration instability caused by traditional belt transmission. The maximum main spin-dle speed can reach 8000 rpm. The main spindle bearing adopts Japan NSK high-precision main axial thrust ball bearing, which ensures the high rotation accuracy, high rigidity and the ability to maintain accuracy of the main spindle.

The linear axis motion pair of this machine tool adopts imported high-speed, high-precision lin-ear guideway and ball screw. It has low friction, high sensitivity and high accuracy stability. It can reach the fast feed speed of 24 m/min while meeting the μ-level precise feed accuracy, fully meets the needs of high-speed machining.

This machine tool standard with 5 side milling live tool, 7 external cutting tools, 4 cross-section drilling tools and 8 back drilling tools to meet the needs of different processing procedures.
This machine tool can be configured with automatic feeder, to realize the continuous feeding of the bar and taking the bar end, so as to realize the fully automatic continuous processing of the machine tool.
The machine tool can be widely used in high-precision, high-speed and efficient mass production of shaft parts in instruments, meters, light industry, electronics, medical equipment and other industries.

Article title: 2 Main Applications and Scope of Use
Article body:
This machine is a high-speed, high-precision CNC lathe with 4-axis interpolated motion control. It integrates mechanical, electrical, and pneumatic systems, capable of automatically performing processes such as external turning, facing, milling, side drilling, grooving, threading, drilling, tapping, boring, and internal threading.

The machine's spindle adopts a built-in motorized spindle structure, eliminating the vibration and instability caused by traditional belt drives.The spindle's maximum speed can reach 6000 rpm. The spindle bearings use high-precision NSK angular contact ball bearings from Japan, ensuring high rotational accuracy, rigidity, and long-term precision retention.

The linear axis motion pairs of the machine use imported high-speed, high-precision ball linear guides and ball screws, offering low friction, high sensitivity, and excellent precision stability. It achieves micron-level feed accuracy while reaching a rapid feed rate of 30 m/min, fully meeting the demands of high-speed machining.

The machine is equipped with 4 side milling live tools, 6 external cutting tools, 4 face drilling tools, and 4 back drilling tools, catering to various processing needs.

By configuring a multi-bar automatic feeder, the machine can achieve continuous loading and unloading of multiple bars, enabling fully automated continuous processing.

This machine is widely applicable to industries such as instruments, light industry, electronics, and medical devices for high-precision, high-speed, and efficient mass production of shaft parts.

Figure 12 Machine Overall Layout Diagram


The overall layout of the machine tool is shown in Figure below.

1.Base 2.Spindle Column 4. Back Drilling Tools 5. Rotary Guide Sleeve 6. Tool Holder 7. Lateral Live Tool 8. Face Drilling Tool Holder 9.Sub Spindle 10. Conveyor Belt 11. Coolant Tank

Base: Primarily supports all structural components.

Column: The column is equipped with X-axis and Y-axis slides and a tool magazine system. The machine tool performs tool changing through the movement of the Y-axis slide. The machine tool achieves radial feed motion of the tool through the up and down movement of the X-axis.

Spindle: The spindle motor is a built-in motor (commonly referred to as an electric spindle), driven by a frequency converter with stepless speed regulation. The spindle housing contains a spindle speed detection encoder. The machine drives the spindle’s front-rear movement via the Z-axis ball screw to achieve axial tool feed.

Pneumatic System: The pneumatic system is located at the lower Back of the machine tool. The machine tool controls the clamping/releasing of the spindle and the extension/retraction of the cylinder through the pneumatic system.

Lubrication System: Located at the lower rear section of the machine,

5.1 Spindle and Sub-Spindle

The machine tool's spindle and sub-spindle are equipped with an integrated motor spindle structure. It is recommended to use Great Wall No.7 spindle oil (or Mobil No.4 spindle oil) for spindle cooling lubrication.

Since the front end of the spindle is protected by an air curtain seal, a slight hissing sound from the front end of the spindle is normal. Adjust the air pressure of the air curtain seal to between 0.2 and0.4 MPa.

Figure 13 Spindle Diagram

Figure 14 Sub-Spindle Diagram

5.2 Feed System

The machine tool has five linear axes: X1, Y1, Z1, X2, and Z2.

The rotational axes include S1 (main spindle), S2 (sub-spindle), S3 (lateral live tool spindle), and S5 (Back live tool spindle). Both S1 (Main Spindle) and S2 (Counter Spindle) are equipped with C-axis functionality. The machine tool system is a dual-channel control system. The first channel includes X1, Y1, Z1, S1 (main spindle), and S3(Lateral Live tool). The second channel includes X2, Z2, S2 (sub-spindle), and S5 (*back tool spindle).

Figure 15 SL325 Tooling Layout 

Figure 16 SL326 Tooling Layout 

5.3 Cooling System

The coolant tank is installed under the right side of the machine base and can be pulled out to the right for easy cleaning. During machining, select the appropriate cutting oil based on the material of the workpiece being processed.

  • Water-soluble cutting oil is not recommended.
  • Do not use cutting oil with high volatility or low flash points.

Figure 17 Diagram of Machine Tool Coolant System

5.4 Pneumatic System

The machine tool's pneumatic system is installed in the pneumatic box at the Back right of the machine.

Adjust the main air supply pressure to approximately 0.5 MPa, ensuring the main air supply is dry and stable. Adjust the air seal pressure to between 0.08 and 0.1 MPa.

Figure 18 Pneumatic System Diagram

5.5 Lubrication System

The machine tool's ball screws and guideways are lubricated through a centralized lubrication system. The lubrication pump is installed on the back of the machine, in a niche on the base. It is recommended to use ISO VG 32lubricating oil. The system can be configured for lubrication duration and intervals. It is recommended to lubricate every 30 minutes for 5 seconds each time (for new machines, the lubrication duration can be appropriately extended).

Lubrication pump oiling time parameter: 3429 (unit: seconds)Lubrication pump oiling interval parameter: 3430 (unit: minutes)

The lubrication pump is equipped with an oil level detection switch. The machine will trigger an alarm when the oil level is too low.

6.3.3 Spindle Chuck Control (M10, M11) – Clamp/Unclamp the Collet Assembly in Spindle

Pneumatic clamp/unclamp control for spindle collet.

The machine tool control panel is equipped with manual spindle collet clamping/unclamping buttons. When the machine operates in automatic mode, these manual buttons are disabled, and spindle collet clamping/unclamping actions are controlled exclusively by the corresponding M-codes.

  1. Command Format

M10      Clamp spindle collet

M11       Unclamp spindle collet

When executing the M11 command during spindle acceleration/deceleration, the spindle unclamping action will commence only after the spindle rotation has fully stopped or dropped below the designated speed threshold.

6.3.4 Sub-Spindle Collet Control (M20, M21)

Clamp or unclamp the sub-spindle collet.

Pneumatic control for sub-spindle collet.

The machine tool control panel is equipped with manual sub-spindle collet clamping/unclamping buttons. When the machine operates in automatic mode, these manual buttons are disabled, and sub-spindle collet clamping/unclamping actions are controlled exclusively by the corresponding M-codes.

  1. Command Format

M20      Sub Spindle Collet Clamp

M21       Unclamp sub-spindle collet

When executing the M21 command during sub-spindle acceleration/deceleration, the spindle unclamping action will commence only after the spindle rotation has fully stopped or dropped below the designated speed threshold.

6.6.5 Coolant Control (M08, M09)

The coolant system of this machine tool is driven by a three-phase motor. When the machine is in manual mode, the coolant is turned on or off by the button switch on the operation panel. When the machine is in automatic operation mode, the coolant is controlled by M codes.

  1. Command Format

M08      Coolant ON

M09      Coolant OFF

6.6.6 HP Coolant Control (Optional)（ M104、M105、M106 、M107、M108、M109、 M110、M111）

This machine tool can be equipped with a high-pressure coolant system, which effectively removes chips wrapped around the cutting tool. The high-pressure coolant system draws cutting oil from the coolant tank. When using the high-pressure coolant system, the fluid level in the tank will be lower than usual. If the coolant tank is filled to capacity, stopping the coolant may cause the tank to overflow. Therefore, when using the high-pressure coolant system, ensure the coolant level is appropriate. The high-pressure coolant system has four coolant circuits. If two or more circuits are opened simultaneously, the fluid pressure from the nozzles may decrease.

The high-pressure coolant circuits are controlled by corresponding M codes.

  1. Command Format

M104        HP Circuit 1 On

M105        HP Circuit 1 Off

M106        HP Circuit 2 On

M107        HP Circuit 2 Off

M108       HP Circuit 3 On

M109        HP Circuit 3 Off

M110         HP Circuit 4 On

M111          HP Circuit 4 Off

6.6.7 Ejector Control (M17, M18)

The extension and retraction of the ejector rod on this machine tool are controlled by a pneumatic valve.

When the machine is in manual mode, the ejector rod can be extended or retracted using the ejector button on the operation panel. When the machine is in automatic machining mode, the corresponding M codes control the extension and retraction of the ejector rod.

  1. Command Format

M17                             Ejector Rod Extend

M18                             Ejector Rod Retract

Note:

  1. If the ejector rod is extended (M17) while the sub-spindle collet is clamped or the sub-spindle is running, an alarm will be triggered.
  2. Do not clamp the sub-spindle (M20) while the ejector rod is extended, as this will trigger an alarm.
  3. The ejector cylinder is equipped with an extension detection switch. If the switch does not detect the relevant signal when the ejector rod is extended (M17), an alarm will be triggered.

6.6.8 Parts Conveyor (M26, M27)

This machine tool is equipped with a parts conveyor that transfers parts ejected from the sub-spindle to an external collection box.

Maximum part diameter: ≤Φ32 mm Maximum part length: ≤ 100 mm Conveyor belt width:         78mm

  1. Command Format

M26                             Parts Conveyor On

M27                             Parts Conveyor Off

Note:

  1. Depending on the part geometry, some parts may not be ejected.
  2. When executing the M26 command, the conveyor belt operates for approximately 15 seconds and then stops automatically. There is no need to use the M27 command to stop the conveyor belt.
  3. The operating time of the conveyor belt can be adjusted by modifying parameter 3432.

6.6.9 Sub-Spindle Air Blow Control (M28, M29)

The sub-spindle air blow function clears chips remaining on the sub-spindle collet.

  1. Command Format

M17                             Sub Spindle Air Blow On

M18                             Sub Spindle Air Blow Off

Note:

  1. The air blow pipeline is equipped with a flow control valve, which may affect the air volume and pressure, impacting the removal of chips from the sub-spindle collet.
  2. Do not remove the flow control valve directly, as this may cause a rapid drop in system pressure when the air blow is activated, affecting the normal operation of the machine. If increased air volume is required, connect an external air source for the sub-spindle air blow.
  3. Simultaneously executing the air blow and ejector rod extension (M17) and retraction (M18) can improve the removal of chips from the sub-spindle collet.
  4. Rotating the sub-spindle at low speed while executing the air blow can also improve chip removal.

6.6.10 Lateral Live Tool Control (M33, M34, M35) Controls the rotation (forward or reverse) and stopping of the lateral live tool.

This machine tool is equipped with four lateral live tools, which can perform lateral milling, lateral drilling, and lateral tapping. Tools T11 can be fitted with ER11 collets, while tools T12, T13 and T14 can be fitted with ER16 collets

  • Command Format

M33 S3=□□□□□ Lateral Live Tool (CW)

M34 S3=□□□□□ Lateral Live Tool (CCW)

M35                 Lateral Live Tool  Stop

Note:

  1. The transmission ratio between the Back live tool spindle and the motor is 1:1.5. The relationship between the tool spindle speed and the motor speed is as follows:
    Tool Spindle Speed=Motor Speed×1.5
  2. 2.When using lateral live tools for machining, the feed rate is typically specified using G98 (feed per minute).
  3. If G99 (feed per revolution) is required to specify the feed rate for live tools or if the lateral live tool tapping function is needed, the M83 code must be executed to designate the live tool spindle as the working spindle.

6.6.11 Back Live Tool Control (Optional) (M93, M94, M95)specifies the rotation (forward or reverse) and stopping of the Back live tools.

This machine can be optionally equipped with 3 Back live tools.  By configuring Back live tools, operations such as Back eccentric hole drilling, Back slot milling, and face milling can be performed. Three rear-mounted live tools are installed on tool stations T32, T33, T34, replacing the standard machine configuration's three rear-mounted drilling tools. The Back live tools are equipped with ER16 collets.

  • Command Format

M93 S5=□□□□□ Back live tool (CW)

M94 S5=□□□□□ Back live tool (CCW)

M95     Back Live Tool Stop

Note:

  1. The transmission ratio between the Back live tool spindle and the motor is 1:2. The relationship between the tool spindle speed and the motor speed is as follows:
    Tool Spindle Speed=Motor Speed×2
  2. When using lateral live tools for machining, the feed rate is typically specified using G98 (feed per minute).
  3. If G99 (feed per revolution) is required to specify the feed rate for live tools or if the lateral live tool tapping function is needed, the M85 code must be executed to designate the live tool spindle as the working spindle.

6.6.12 Designate Working Spindle (M81, M82, M83, M85)

By default, the machine system designates the first channel spindle as the working spindle, and the second channel sub-spindle as the working spindle. When performing operations such as lateral tapping, Back tapping, or interpolation with other spindles, the corresponding M code must be executed to designate the appropriate spindle as the working spindle.

After completing machining with another designated working spindle, the corresponding M code must be executed to switch the working spindle back to the main spindle or sub-spindle.

Command Format

M81     Designate main spindle as working spindle

M82     Designate sub-spindle as working spindle

M83     Designate lateral live tool spindle as working spindle

M85     Designate Back live tool spindle as working spindle

6.6.13 Automatic Cut-Off（G266      A* X* S* F*)

During the machining process, if an abnormal condition occurs (e.g., tool life expiration, tool breakage, machine fault), the machine must be stopped. After resolving the issue, a cut-off operation is required before restarting the machining process. The G266 command can be used to perform an automatic cut-off operation.

Command Format (G266   A* X* S* F*)

A: Quick Positioning Position – Bar Diameter Value (mm) X: Cut-off Feed Position – Parting Diameter Value (mm) S: Spindle Speed for Cut-off (RPM)

F: Feed per Revolution (mm/rev)

The G266 command implements automatic cut-off through a pre-packaged macro program (G266). The macro logic is structured as follows:

$1

%         @MACRO      // G266 A* X* S* F* Yongbo 20210418 WAIT();

IF #1 =#0 THEN

ALARM(100,"G266 A* DATA ERROR");      //A OD Data Error END_IF;

IF #9 =#0 THEN

ALARM(101,"G266 F* DATA ERROR");       //F Feed Command Error END_IF;

IF #19 =#0 THEN

ALARM(102,"G266 S* DATA ERROR");      //S Spindle Speed Error END_IF;

IF #24 =#0 THEN

ALARM(103,"G266 X* DATA ERROR");      //X Cut-off Position Error END_IF;

WAIT();

N000;

IF(#1036=1) THEN     //T code Verification GOTO100;

ELSE;

T0101;             //Tool Call T0101

END_IF;

N100;

IF (@10721<=#24*1000) THEN        //X-axis Mechanical Position

IF (@10722=0.0) THEN         //Y-axis Mechanical Position GOTO999;

END_IF;

END_IF;

N800;

M10;

M08;

G28 U0.0;       //X-axis Safe Position

G00 Y0.0;        //Y-axis Safe Position

M03 S#19;      //M03 Spindle CW Rotation

G00 X(#1+2.0);           //X-axis Rapid Positioning

G99 G01 X#24 F#9;   //X-axis Cut-off Position M05;

M09;

N999;

M99;

$2

M99;

Note:

  1. T01 is predefined as the cut-off tool. Using any tool other than T01 will trigger axis collision risks due to unmapped tool offsets.
  2. T01 must be manually positioned above the workpiece at a safe Z-height before executing codes.

6.6.14 Dual-Spindle Synchronization Function (G200, G113)

Synchronizes the speed and phase of the main spindle and sub-spindle. In this mode, the machine can perform the following operations:

  1. Transferring a workpiece from the main spindle to the sub-spindle.
  2. Simultaneously holding and machining a single workpiece with both the main spindle and sub-spindle.
  3. Maintaining phase synchronization between the main spindle and sub-spindle for machining irregularly shaped parts.

Command Format

G200   S□□□□             Synchronizes the main spindle and sub-spindle to rotate at speed S□□□□.

G113 Cancels the dual-spindle synchronization function

6.6.15 Dual Z-Axis Coupling Function (M1000, M1001)

Couples the Z2 axis with the Z1 axis, enabling the Z2 axis to follow the Z1 axis in synchronized motion. In this mode, the machine can perform the following operations:

  1. Simultaneously holding and performing external turning on a single workpiece with the main spindle and sub-spindle.
  2. Simultaneously holding and performing side milling on a single workpiece with the main spindle and sub-spindle.
  3. Simultaneously holding and performing external thread turning on a single workpiece with the main spindle and sub-spindle.

Command Format

M1000         Enables Z1 and Z2 axis coupling

M1001         Decouple Z1/Z2 Axis Synchronization

6.6.16 Dual C-Axis Coupling Function (M1002, M1003) Same as above

System Wait Function (G04.1)

Command Format

G04.1 P□□

P□□ Wait signal code.

The numbers following P must be assigned in ascending order.

G04.1 P□□  must be referenced equally across axis groups to prevent motion desynchronization. If machining is performed using a single axis, the system wait code (G04.1 P□□) is ignored.
